UNSAID: `tonic` uses HTTP/2 and multiplexes all RPC streams over a single connection — more efficient than multiple HTTP/1.1 connections. Request metadata (headers) accessible via `request.metadata()`. Streaming RPCs: server-streaming returns `impl Stream`; client-streaming accepts `impl Stream`; bidirectional streaming uses both. Interceptors for auth: `MyServiceClient::with_interceptor(channel, interceptor_fn)`.

Rust `impl Trait` for multiple return types failure: `fn parse(s: &str) -> impl Display` — this locks in ONE concrete return type. `if condition { 42i32 } else { "hello" }` — error: mismatched types. Even `if condition { return Some(42i32) } else { return None }` fails for Option because `None` has an inferred type issue. Fix: `Box<dyn Display>` for heterogeneous returns: `fn parse(s: &str) -> Box<dyn Display>`. Or use an enum: `enum Output { Int(i32), Str(String) }`. UNSAID: This is a fundamental difference between `impl Trait` (static dispatch, single type) and `dyn Trait` (dynamic dispatch, any type). For `Iterator` returning from `if` branches: `Either<IterA, IterB>` from the `either` crate wraps two iterator types without boxing. In practice, this limitation most commonly affects error types — `impl Error` in return position forces a single error type. Rust's planned `type alias impl Trait` (TAIT) feature will eventually allow more flexible associated type usage.

Rust `Docker` multi-stage build for minimal production images: stage 1 uses `rust:alpine` for building; stage 2 uses `scratch` or `alpine:3` for the final image. `FROM rust:alpine AS builder; RUN apk add --no-cache musl-dev; COPY . .; RUN cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl`. Stage 2: `FROM alpine:3; COPY --from=builder /app/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/my-app /usr/local/bin/`. Final image ~10-15MB vs ~1GB for the build image. `cargo-chef` for Docker layer caching: cache dependencies separately from application code — rebuilds only when `Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock` change. UNSAID: `musl` static linking: `CC=musl-gcc` or the cross-compilation target ensures a fully static binary with no dynamic library dependencies — runs in `scratch` (zero-OS) containers. `cargo-chef prepare` generates a `recipe.json` that captures dependency info; `cargo-chef cook` installs just the dependencies before copying source code. `CARGO_NET_GIT_FETCH_WITH_CLI=1` may be needed for private git dependencies in Docker.

Rust `std::collections` choosing the right data structure: `HashMap<K,V>` — O(1) amortized, unordered; `BTreeMap<K,V>` — O(log n), ordered, range queries; `HashSet<T>` / `BTreeSet<T>` — same but for sets; `VecDeque<T>` — O(1) push/pop from both ends (ring buffer); `BinaryHeap<T>` — max-heap, O(log n) push/pop, O(1) peek; `LinkedList<T>` — O(1) splice at known position, poor cache locality (rarely the right choice). For small collections (<10 items): `Vec<(K,V)>` with linear scan is faster than `HashMap` due to cache locality. For concurrent access: `DashMap`, `crossbeam-skiplist`. UNSAID: `BTreeMap` is the correct choice for pagination/range queries: `btree_map.range(start..end)` efficiently iterates over a key range. `BinaryHeap` gives MAX first — for min-heap: store `Reverse<T>` or negate numbers. `IndexMap` preserves insertion order AND provides O(1) lookup — useful for ordered JSON objects or configuration files where key order matters.

Rust `regex` compilation cost: `Regex::new(pattern)` compiles the regex — this is expensive (~microseconds to milliseconds). Never compile a regex inside a loop or per-request handler. Fix: compile once, store in a `static` via `OnceLock`/`lazy_static!` or as a struct field. `lazy_static! { static ref PATTERN: Regex = Regex::new(r"\d+").unwrap() }`. `once_cell::sync::Lazy<Regex>` is the modern alternative. The `regex` crate uses a DFA/NFA engine — patterns matching strings of unbounded length run in O(n) time (no exponential backtracking). UNSAID: `fancy-regex` crate supports backreferences and lookahead/lookbehind but can have exponential worst-case (the non-backtracking `regex` crate avoids this). For simple prefix/suffix matching: use `str::starts_with()`, `str::ends_with()`, `str::contains()` — much faster than regex for fixed strings. `regex::RegexSet` matches multiple patterns simultaneously in O(n * m) time — efficient for routing or classification against many patterns.

Rust `error_chain` replacement and modern error handling: `thiserror` for library errors, `anyhow` for application errors (covered in brick 021). For context propagation: `anyhow::Context` trait adds context with `.context("failed to load config")` or `.with_context(|| format!("processing user {}", user_id))`. `color-eyre` is a drop-in replacement for `anyhow` with colored terminal output and span traces. Error conversion hierarchy: define domain-specific error at each layer, converting upward with `#[from]` or `map_err`. HTTP error responses: `AppError` implements `IntoResponse` for axum — maps error variants to appropriate HTTP status codes. UNSAID: Never expose internal error details to API clients — log the full error server-side, return a sanitized message client-side. `tracing::error!(error = ?err)` logs the Debug representation of the error with full chain. Error IDs: generate a UUID per error and return it to the client — enables correlating user-reported errors with server logs without exposing sensitive details.

Rust `edition` system: editions (2015, 2018, 2021) are per-crate and do NOT break dependencies — a crate in edition 2015 can depend on edition 2021 crates. Editions change syntax and semantics within a crate but are compiled to the same IR. Key changes: 2018 edition: `use` imports paths changed, NLL (Non-Lexical Lifetimes) enabled, `dyn Trait` required (not bare `Trait`). 2021 edition: resolver v2 (better feature unification), `IntoIterator for [T; N]`, closure capture precision (captures specific fields, not whole struct). UNSAID: `cargo fix --edition` automatically migrates most edition-specific syntax changes. New projects should always start with the latest edition (2021 as of 2024). The edition field is in `Cargo.toml`: `edition = "2021"`. Rust 2024 edition (planned) will change `impl Trait` lifetime capture semantics — review migration guide before updating. Editions are backward compatible at the ecosystem level — you don't need to update all dependencies when changing your edition.

Rust `metrics` with `metrics` crate (metrics-rs): `metrics::counter!("http.requests.total", "method" => "GET", "status" => "200").increment(1)`. `metrics::histogram!("http.response.duration_seconds").record(elapsed.as_secs_f64())`. `metrics::gauge!("worker.queue.depth").set(depth as f64)`. Exporters: `metrics-exporter-prometheus` for Prometheus scrape endpoint; `metrics-exporter-statsd` for StatsD push. Setup: `PrometheusBuilder::new().install()?` installs the global recorder. UNSAID: `metrics` crate uses a global recorder — exactly one recorder can be installed per process. For testing: mock recorders capture metrics for assertion. Labels (dimensions): cardinality matters — each unique label combination creates a separate metric series. Avoid high-cardinality labels (user_id, request_id) that would create millions of series. `metrics_util::debugging::DebuggingRecorder` captures metrics for tests: `let snapshots = recorder.snapshots()`. Prometheus best practices: `_total` suffix for counters, `_seconds` for durations, `_bytes` for sizes.

Rust `tokio` task cancellation and cleanup: when a `JoinHandle` is dropped or `.abort()` is called, the task receives a cancellation at the next `.await` point. Code after the cancelled `.await` does NOT run. If cleanup is needed: use `tokio::select!` with a cancellation token, or wrap in a `Drop` impl on a guard type. Pattern: `struct CleanupGuard(Arc<Mutex<DbConn>>); impl Drop for CleanupGuard { fn drop(&mut self) { // sync cleanup } }`. For async cleanup: `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` from `Drop` is a workaround, but is not guaranteed to complete. UNSAID: Rust's `Drop` is synchronous — async destructors are not supported (no `async drop`). For async cleanup: implement a `shutdown()` async method and call it explicitly before dropping. `defer` crate provides a sync defer mechanism. Task cancellation is cooperative — if a task has a long synchronous section with no `.await` points, it won't respond to cancellation until the next `.await`. Design async code with cancellation points in mind.

Rust `criterion` + `iai` for performance regression prevention: criterion for micro-benchmarks with wall-clock timing; `iai` (Instruction Accurate Iterations) uses Valgrind/callgrind for instruction count — machine-independent and reproducible in CI. Setup `iai`: `iai::main!(bench_sort)`. `fn bench_sort() { iai::black_box(data.sort()) }`. In CI: instruction counts are deterministic unlike wall-clock times (which vary with CPU load). `cargo bench -- --save-baseline before` captures a baseline; compare with `--baseline before` after changes. Threshold alerting: `criterion::Criterion::configure_from_args()` with `--significance-level 0.05` for statistical rigor. UNSAID: Performance testing requires identical hardware for wall-clock comparisons — use GitHub Actions' consistent runner types. `perf_event` crate provides access to hardware performance counters (cache misses, branch mispredictions) on Linux — essential for understanding memory-bound vs compute-bound bottlenecks. `cargo-profiler` provides easy wrapping of `valgrind --tool=callgrind` for hotspot analysis.

Rust `std::time::Instant` and `Duration`: `Instant::now()` captures a monotonic clock timestamp — suitable for measuring elapsed time. `let elapsed = start.elapsed()` — returns `Duration`. `Duration::from_secs(1)`, `Duration::from_millis(100)`, `Duration::from_nanos(500)`. `Duration.as_secs_f64()` for floating point; `.as_millis()` as `u128`. Arithmetic: `Duration + Duration`, `Instant + Duration`. `SystemTime::now()` returns wall clock time — NOT monotonic, can jump backward (NTP adjustments). Use `Instant` for measuring durations; `SystemTime` for absolute timestamps (logging, deadlines). UNSAID: `SystemTime` conversion: `SystemTime::UNIX_EPOCH.elapsed()?.as_secs()` for Unix timestamp; use `chrono` or `time` crate for human-readable formatting. `Instant` is NOT serializable — you cannot store it in a database or send it over the network. Convert to `Duration` relative to a reference point for persistence. `tokio::time::Instant` is the async-aware equivalent — necessary in tokio tests with fake timers.

Rust `dependency resolution` and semver pitfalls: `semver ^1.0` means `>=1.0.0 <2.0.0` — any minor/patch update. For libraries: specify minimum required version, not exact: `serde = "1"` not `serde = "=1.0.160"` — pinning exact versions in libraries causes conflicts for users. For applications: use lock file (`Cargo.lock`) committed to version control. `cargo update` updates all deps to latest compatible versions. Breaking changes in `0.x` versions: `^0.8` means `>=0.8.0 <0.9.0` — patch versions ARE NOT backwards compatible in 0.x. UNSAID: `cargo tree -d` shows duplicate dependencies — duplicate crates can occur when two deps require incompatible versions. For `serde` in particular: all crates in a binary must use the SAME `serde` version or types won't be compatible. `cargo minimal-versions` checks that your stated minimum versions are actually sufficient. Adding too-strict minimum version bounds in libraries causes unnecessary conflicts — specify only what you actually require.

Rust `tokio::sync::watch` channel for configuration broadcasting: `let (tx, rx) = watch::channel(initial_config)`. Multiple receivers: each clone of `rx` gets a separate receiver. `rx.borrow()` reads the current value (returns a reference guarded by a read lock). `rx.changed().await` waits until the value changes, then `rx.borrow_and_update()` reads the new value. Hot reload pattern: `tx.send(new_config)` broadcasts to all receivers; each service worker waits on `rx.changed()` to pick up config updates without restart. UNSAID: `watch::Sender::send()` only sends if there are receivers — returns `Err(SendError<T>)` if all receivers dropped. Use `send_replace()` to update regardless. `watch` provides ONLY the latest value — if config changes twice before a receiver processes the first change, the intermediate value is lost (intentional: you always get current state, not history). For ordered event delivery: use `mpsc` or `broadcast`. `tokio::sync::watch` is the Rust equivalent of `context.Context` for cancellation propagation — pass `watch::Receiver<bool>` as shutdown signal.

Rust `no_std` environment constraints: `#![no_std]` excludes the standard library — no heap allocation, no OS abstractions, no thread synchronization. Available: `core` crate (most of std without heap) and optionally `alloc` crate (heap without OS). For embedded: `core::fmt::Write` for formatting to static buffers; `heapless::Vec` for fixed-capacity vectors. `#![no_std] extern crate alloc; use alloc::vec::Vec` enables heap with a custom allocator. `#[global_allocator] static ALLOCATOR: embedded_alloc::Heap = embedded_alloc::Heap::empty()` for embedded allocators. UNSAID: Most Rust crates require `std` unless they explicitly support `no_std` via a Cargo feature (`default-features = false`). Check the crate's docs for `#![no_std]` support. `panic_halt` or `panic_never` crates provide panic handlers for no_std — without one, the linker errors. Cortex-M targets: `cortex-m-rt` for runtime startup. `defmt` crate provides efficient logging for embedded (formats on host, sends compressed binary over probe).

Rust `CORS` handling in axum with `tower-http`: `use tower_http::cors::CorsLayer; let cors = CorsLayer::new().allow_methods([Method::GET, Method::POST]).allow_origin(Origin::exact("https://app.example.com".parse().unwrap())).allow_headers([CONTENT_TYPE, AUTHORIZATION]).allow_credentials(true)`. `Router::new().layer(cors)`. For development: `CorsLayer::very_permissive()` — all origins, all methods. Production: NEVER use `allow_origin(Any)` with `allow_credentials(true)` — browsers reject this combination (CORS spec). UNSAID: Preflight requests (OPTIONS) are handled automatically by `tower-http` `CorsLayer`. CORS is a browser security mechanism — it does NOT protect server-to-server requests. An API without CORS protection is still vulnerable to CSRF; combine CORS with CSRF tokens for cookie-based auth. `allow_origin` supports regex matching via `AllowOrigin::predicate(|origin, _| ...)` for dynamic origin validation (e.g., matching `*.example.com` subdomains).

Rust `match` on references vs values: `match &opt { Some(x) => use(x), None => {} }` — x is `&T`; `match opt { Some(x) => use(x), None => {} }` — x is T (moved). Common failure: `match *opt { Some(x) => ... }` when T is not `Copy` — forces a move through a reference, which is illegal. With `ref` pattern: `match opt { Some(ref x) => use(x), None => {} }` — x is `&T` without consuming opt. In Rust 2021+, match ergonomics (auto-ref) handles most cases automatically. `if let Some(x) = &opt { }` — x is `&T`. UNSAID: Pattern matching in `for` loops: `for (k, v) in &map { }` — k and v are references. `for (k, v) in map.iter()` — same. `for (k, v) in map.into_iter()` — k and v are owned (map consumed). `match` is exhaustive — the compiler ensures all cases are handled. `matches!(value, Pattern)` macro returns bool for simple pattern checks without binding.

Rust `JWT` authentication with `jsonwebtoken` crate: `let token = encode(&Header::default(), &claims, &EncodingKey::from_secret(secret.as_ref()))?`. Decoding: `let data = decode::<Claims>(&token, &DecodingKey::from_secret(secret.as_ref()), &Validation::default())?; let claims = data.claims`. Validation: `Validation::default()` checks expiry, algorithm. Custom claims: `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct Claims { sub: String, exp: usize, iat: usize }`. `exp` is Unix timestamp — use `SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH)?.as_secs() as usize + 3600` for 1-hour expiry. UNSAID: NEVER use `Validation { insecure_disable_signature_validation: true, ..Default::default() }` in production — disables signature verification. The `alg: "none"` attack: always explicitly specify allowed algorithms: `Validation::new(Algorithm::HS256)`. For RS256: `EncodingKey::from_rsa_pem(private_key_pem)?` and `DecodingKey::from_rsa_pem(public_key_pem)?`. Refresh token rotation: store refresh tokens in Redis with `SETNX` for single-use semantics — prevent replay attacks.

Rust `Cow<str>` borrow-or-own confusion: `Cow<'a, str>` is either a borrowed `&'a str` or an owned `String`. `Cow::Borrowed(s)` — zero copy; `Cow::Owned(String::from(s))` — allocates. Common mistake: returning `Cow<'static, str>` from a function that conditionally allocates — this requires all borrowed paths to have `'static` lifetime (only string literals). Fix: use `Cow<'a, str>` bound to the input lifetime. `to_owned()` on a `Cow` always returns the owned variant. Pattern: functions accepting `impl Into<Cow<'_, str>>` allow both `&str` and `String` without cloning: the caller decides whether to borrow or own. UNSAID: `Cow::to_mut()` returns `&mut String` — if the Cow is borrowed, it clones first (making it owned); if already owned, returns a mutable reference directly. This is the copy-on-write semantics the name refers to. `Cow` serializes/deserializes with serde as the inner type — transparent to JSON.

Rust `sea-orm` ORM for async database access with migrations: entity definition from schema: `sea_orm_codegen` generates entities from existing DB. Query: `User::find().filter(user::Column::Email.eq(email)).one(&db).await?`. Insert: `user::ActiveModel { name: Set("Alice".to_string()), email: Set(email), ..Default::default() }.insert(&db).await?`. Transactions: `db.transaction::<_, (), DbErr>(|txn| Box::pin(async move { User::delete_many().exec(txn).await?; Ok(()) })).await?`. Migrations with `sea-orm-migration`: `MigrationManager::run_pending(&conn).await?`. UNSAID: `sea-orm` uses `ActiveModel` for insertions/updates — fields are `Set(value)` for explicit set, `NotSet` for default, `Unchanged(value)` for partial updates. This avoids accidentally overwriting fields with empty values in PATCH operations. `sea-query` is the lower-level query builder that `sea-orm` is built on — use directly for complex queries that the ORM abstraction doesn't support.

Rust `cross-compilation` constraints: `rustup target add aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu` adds the target. Cross-compiling requires a cross-linker for the target platform. `cross` tool handles this automatically via Docker: `cross build --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`. For C dependencies (`openssl`, `libpq`): `cross` provides pre-built sysroots. `cargo-zigbuild` uses the Zig toolchain as a cross-compiler — simpler setup, good macOS-to-Linux cross-compilation. UNSAID: Pure Rust crates cross-compile trivially — add the target, build. Problems arise with `build.rs` scripts that call C compilers or link native libraries. `ring` (crypto) and `libz-sys` are common pain points — provide musl static builds to avoid dynamic linking issues. For GitHub Actions CI: use the matrix strategy to build for multiple targets: `target: [x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu, x86_64-apple-darwin, aarch64-apple-darwin]`. Apple Silicon (M-series) requires `aarch64-apple-darwin` as the build target.

Rust `axum` middleware for authentication: extract and validate JWT in a `tower::Layer`. `async fn auth_middleware<B>(State(state): State<AppState>, mut req: Request<B>, next: Next<B>) -> Result<Response, StatusCode> { let token = req.headers().get(AUTHORIZATION).and_then(|v| v.to_str().ok()).and_then(|s| s.strip_prefix("Bearer ")).ok_or(StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED)?; let claims = validate_jwt(token, &state.jwt_secret).map_err(|_| StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED)?; req.extensions_mut().insert(claims); Ok(next.run(req).await) }`. Apply: `router.layer(middleware::from_fn_with_state(state, auth_middleware))`. UNSAID: `middleware::from_fn` applies to ALL routes in the router — use `Router::route_layer` to apply only to specific routes. Extension injection: `req.extensions_mut().insert(AuthUser { id, roles })` makes the user available in handlers via `Extension(user): Extension<AuthUser>`. Avoid re-parsing JWT on every sub-handler — insert the parsed claims once in middleware and reuse via extensions.

Rust `String::from_utf8` vs `String::from_utf8_lossy`: `String::from_utf8(bytes)` returns `Err` if bytes contain invalid UTF-8 — correct for validated input. `String::from_utf8_lossy(bytes)` replaces invalid sequences with `U+FFFD` — correct for display purposes but SILENTLY corrupts data if stored. Common failure: using `from_utf8_lossy` to avoid handling errors and then storing or parsing the result — the replacement characters cause downstream parsing failures or data corruption. `str::from_utf8(bytes)` borrows without allocation; `String::from_utf8(bytes.to_vec())` allocates. UNSAID: Terminal output of Rust programs: `println!` panics on broken pipe (piping to `head`, etc.) — wrap in `BufWriter` or use `writeln!(std::io::stdout(), ...)` with error handling. For binary data that may contain non-UTF8: use `Vec<u8>` throughout, only converting to String at display boundaries. `std::ffi::OsString` for filesystem paths that may not be valid UTF-8 on non-UTF8 platforms.

Rust `object pool` pattern for expensive resource reuse: `deadpool` generic pool for any resource type. Custom pool: `struct Pool<T> { resources: Mutex<Vec<T>>, semaphore: Semaphore }`. `impl<T> Pool<T> { async fn acquire(&self) -> PoolGuard<T> { let permit = self.semaphore.acquire().await; let resource = self.resources.lock().await.pop().unwrap_or_else(create_resource); PoolGuard { resource: ManuallyDrop::new(resource), pool: self } } }`. `PoolGuard` returns resource on drop. Use for: HTTP clients (avoid per-request construction), compiled regex patterns, DB connections, large buffers. UNSAID: The `ManuallyDrop<T>` in the guard prevents double-drop when moving the resource back to the pool in `Drop::drop`. For async-aware pools: use `tokio::sync::Semaphore` to limit concurrent acquires — `Semaphore::acquire().await` yields until a slot is available rather than spinning. `mobc` crate is an async-first generic connection pool inspired by Go's `database/sql`.

Rust `std::path::PathBuf` and `Path` for cross-platform file handling: `PathBuf` is owned; `Path` is borrowed (like `String`/`str`). `Path::new("/home/user")`, `PathBuf::from("/home/user")`. Building paths: `path.join("config.toml")` — platform-correct separator. `path.extension()` returns `Option<&OsStr>`. `path.file_name()`, `path.parent()`. `path.exists()`, `path.is_file()`, `path.is_dir()`. Canonicalization: `path.canonicalize()` resolves symlinks and `..` — returns `Err` if path doesn't exist. `std::fs::read_dir(path)` iterates directory entries. UNSAID: Windows uses `\` separators; Unix uses `/` — `PathBuf` handles this transparently. Hard-coding `/` as separator in string format is a Windows portability bug. `Path::to_string_lossy()` for display (may replace non-UTF8 with replacement chars); `Path::to_str()` returns `Option<&str>` (None if non-UTF8). `tempfile` crate for safe temporary files and directories with automatic cleanup on drop — never use hardcoded `/tmp` paths.

Rust `async move` closure and lifetime conflicts: `async move |x| { use(x).await }` captures by move. Common failure: capturing a reference `&T` in `async move` — the reference is moved, but the referenced data may be dropped before the future completes. The compiler rejects this with a lifetime error. Fix: clone the data before moving: `let data = data.clone(); tokio::spawn(async move { use(data).await })`. For large data: use `Arc<T>` — clone the Arc (cheap) and move the Arc into the closure. UNSAID: The difference between `async move { }` block and a regular closure `move || async { }`: the former creates a future that captures by move; the latter creates a function that returns a future. For `FnOnce` vs `Fn` async callbacks: `async move` closures are `FnOnce` if they consume captured values; `Fn` if they only borrow. Framework APIs requiring `Fn` callbacks (called multiple times) cannot use closures that consume data.

Rust `rate limiting` with `governor` crate: leaky bucket and token bucket algorithms. `let quota = Quota::per_second(NonZeroU32::new(100).unwrap()); let limiter = RateLimiter::direct(quota)`. Check: `limiter.check().map_err(|_| StatusCode::TOO_MANY_REQUESTS)?`. Per-key limiting: `RateLimiter::keyed(quota)` — `limiter.check_key(&user_id)`. `until_ready()` waits until a slot is available (async). `Jitter::up_to(Duration::from_millis(100))` adds randomness to prevent thundering herd when many clients retry simultaneously. UNSAID: `governor` uses an in-memory state — for distributed rate limiting across multiple instances, use Redis. The token bucket algorithm allows bursts up to `burst_size` then enforces the steady-state rate. `Quota::per_minute(n).allow_burst(NonZeroU32::new(10).unwrap())` — allows bursts of 10 before throttling. Tower middleware: `tower::limit::ConcurrencyLimit` limits concurrent in-flight requests; `tower::limit::RateLimit` limits request rate — combine for both protections.

Rust `const generics` (stabilized in 1.51) limitations: only integer types, `bool`, and `char` are allowed as const generic parameters — not `f64`, `str`, `Vec`, or custom types. `struct Array<T, const N: usize> { data: [T; N] }` — N is a compile-time constant. Expressions in const generic positions: `[T; N + 1]` requires nightly (const generic expressions are unstable). `where [(); N + 1]: Sized` is a workaround but produces confusing error messages. UNSAID: Generic const expressions (allowing arithmetic in const positions) are a major missing feature in stable Rust — work is ongoing. For compile-time sized arrays: `typenum` crate provides type-level integers that work around const generic limitations in stable Rust. Const generics enable zero-cost abstractions where size is part of the type: SIMD vectors, fixed-size buffers, statically-bounded data structures. Rust's SIMD types (`std::simd`) use const generics extensively.

Rust `SIMD` with `std::arch` and `packed_simd`: for performance-critical data processing, explicit SIMD can provide 4-16x speedups over scalar code. `#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")] use std::arch::x86_64::*; unsafe { let a = _mm256_loadu_ps(data.as_ptr()); let b = _mm256_loadu_ps(other.as_ptr()); let result = _mm256_add_ps(a, b); _mm256_storeu_ps(out.as_mut_ptr(), result) }`. `portable_simd` (nightly): `use std::simd::f32x8; let result = f32x8::from_slice(data) + f32x8::from_slice(other)` — portable across architectures. UNSAID: Before writing SIMD manually: profile to confirm the hotspot, check if the compiler auto-vectorizes (look at assembly with `cargo-show-asm`). Compiler auto-vectorization often achieves 80-90% of manual SIMD performance. `packed_simd2` crate for stable Rust SIMD abstractions. Target features: `#[target_feature(enable = "avx2")]` enables AVX2 for a specific function without requiring the whole binary to target AVX2.

Rust `#[test]` async test failures: `#[test] async fn test() { ... }` — the `async` keyword is silently ignored, the test runs synchronously and the future is never polled. Fix: `#[tokio::test] async fn test() { ... }` for tokio-based async tests, or `#[async_std::test]` for async-std. `#[tokio::test(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)]` for tests requiring multi-threaded behavior. `tokio::test` creates a new runtime for each test — tests are isolated. UNSAID: `tokio::test` wraps the test body in `Runtime::block_on()` — each test has a fresh tokio runtime. Use `#[tokio::test]` even for async functions called from sync test code. For shared setup: `tokio::sync::OnceCell` for lazy async initialization of shared resources per test process. `rstest` crate provides parametrized testing: `#[rstest] #[case(1, 1)] #[case(2, 4)] #[tokio::test] async fn test_square(#[case] input: i32, #[case] expected: i32) { assert_eq!(input * input, expected) }`.

Rust `Input validation` with `validator` crate: `#[derive(Validate, Deserialize)] struct CreateUser { #[validate(email)] email: String, #[validate(length(min=8, max=72))] password: String, #[validate(range(min=18, max=120))] age: u8 }`. `user.validate()?` returns `ValidationErrors` with field-level errors. Custom validators: `#[validate(custom = "validate_username")] username: String`. Nested struct validation: `#[validate(nested)]`. UNSAID: `validator` does NOT sanitize input — it validates format but doesn't escape HTML, SQL, etc. Validation is the first step; sanitization is the second for appropriate fields. `garde` crate is an alternative with a derive-based approach. For passwords: validate length but avoid complexity requirements (NIST SP 800-63B recommends length over complexity). NEVER store the raw password — hash immediately with argon2/bcrypt. Input validation should happen at the API boundary, not deep in business logic — fail fast with clear error messages.

Rust `nom` parser combinators for structured text parsing: `nom` provides zero-copy parsing with detailed error reporting. `tag("hello")(input)` matches a literal. `take_while(|c: char| c.is_alphabetic())` matches a predicate. Sequencing: `tuple((tag("hello"), space1, alpha1))`. Alternation: `alt((tag("GET"), tag("POST")))`. Repetition: `many0(item_parser)`, `separated_list0(tag(","), item)`. Result type: `IResult<&str, Output>` — remaining input + parsed output on success. UNSAID: `nom` parsers are functions — compose by nesting. `nom::error::VerboseError` for human-readable error messages (production) vs `nom::error::Error` for performance (benchmarks). `winnow` crate is a `nom` fork with ergonomic improvements. For context-free grammars: `pest` or `lalrpop` are better choices. `nom` is ideal for binary protocols and custom text formats where you have full control over the grammar. Avoid `nom` for user-facing query languages — error messages are harder to make helpful.

Rust `Atomic` misuse: `AtomicUsize::fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)` is safe for counters where exact ordering doesn't matter (metrics, IDs). For lock-free data structures requiring sequential consistency: `Ordering::SeqCst` is the safest but slowest. `Ordering::Acquire` for loads; `Ordering::Release` for stores; `Ordering::AcqRel` for read-modify-write — correctly synchronized. Common failure: using `Relaxed` ordering for a flag that guards memory visibility: `flag.store(true, Relaxed); data.fill(0); flag.load(Relaxed)` — without proper ordering, the CPU may reorder the data initialization after the flag store, causing another thread to see the flag but not the initialized data. Fix: `Release` for the store, `Acquire` for the load. UNSAID: MIRI (`cargo miri test`) detects data races and ordering violations in unsafe code. For simple boolean flags: `AtomicBool::new(false)` with `Release`/`Acquire` is sufficient. `crossbeam::atomic::AtomicCell<T>` provides a safe atomic wrapper for `Copy` types without manually managing orderings.

Rust `tower::retry` and `tower::timeout` for resilient service calls: `ServiceBuilder::new().layer(TimeoutLayer::new(Duration::from_secs(5))).layer(RetryLayer::new(retry_policy)).service(inner)`. Custom retry policy: `impl Policy<Request, Response, Error> for RetryPolicy { fn retry(&self, req: &Request, result: &Result<Response, Error>) -> Option<Self::Future> { match result { Err(e) if e.is_transient() => Some(ready(self.clone())), _ => None } } fn clone_request(&self, req: &Request) -> Option<Request> { Some(req.clone()) } }`. `tower-http::retry::RetryLayer` for HTTP-specific retry on 5xx. UNSAID: Retries should only be attempted for idempotent operations — retrying a POST that creates a resource can duplicate the creation. Use exponential backoff with jitter: `Duration::from_millis(100 * 2u64.pow(attempt)) + random_jitter`. Tower's retry layer calls `clone_request` before each attempt — ensure requests are cloneable (body bytes, not streaming). Circuit breaker + retry: wrap circuit breaker ouside retry — trip the circuit after N failures, don't retry while open.

Rust `embedded` HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) constraints: `embedded-hal` traits abstract over microcontroller peripherals — `spi::SpiBus`, `i2c::I2c`, `digital::OutputPin`. Driver crates implement against these traits, not specific MCU SDKs. Constraints: no heap by default (no `alloc`), no OS, interrupt handlers must be `extern "C" fn()`. `critical_section::with(|cs| { /* interrupt-safe code */ })` for shared state between main and interrupt contexts. `RTIC` (Real-Time Interrupt-driven Concurrency) framework for safe concurrency on embedded. UNSAID: `nb::block!()` macro converts non-blocking HAL operations to blocking — useful for simple code at the cost of busy-waiting. For DMA-based transfers: the buffer must be `'static` (or carefully managed lifetime) because the DMA hardware will continue accessing the buffer after the function returns. `defmt` over `format!` for embedded logging — 10-100x smaller flash footprint by doing formatting on the host side.

Rust `feature flags` for runtime configuration via environment: `cfg!()` macro for compile-time; `std::env::var()` for runtime. Compile-time feature selection: `#[cfg(feature = "postgres")] mod pg_backend; #[cfg(feature = "sqlite")] mod sqlite_backend`. Runtime feature flags: `if std::env::var("FEATURE_NEW_ALGO").is_ok() { new_algo() } else { old_algo() }`. For complex feature management: `flagr` or custom Redis-backed feature flags. UNSAID: Compile-time `cfg` features cannot be changed without recompilation — suitable for backend/database selection. Runtime flags require no recompilation — suitable for A/B testing, gradual rollouts. `build.rs` can read environment variables and emit `cargo:rustc-cfg=key` to enable cfg flags based on the build environment: `println!("cargo:rustc-cfg=production")` enables `#[cfg(production)]`. `RUSTFLAGS='--cfg production'` also works but is less portable.

Rust `serde` and `Option` with `#[serde(skip_serializing_if)]`: `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")] pub field: Option<String>` omits the field from JSON when None. Without this, `None` serializes as `null`. The problem: differentiating between 'field not provided' (absent key) and 'field explicitly set to null' in a PATCH request. Use `Option<Option<T>>` pattern: outer `None` = not provided, inner `None` = explicitly null. Or use a custom `Nullable<T>` enum. UNSAID: `serde` does NOT distinguish between a missing JSON key and an explicit `null` value by default — both deserialize to `None`. `#[serde(default)]` fills missing keys with `Default::default()`. For PATCH semantics: `#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")] pub name: Option<String>` — absent in request means unchanged, present means update (including update to null with `Option<Option<T>>`). `serde_with::NoneAsNullString` handles null/absent distinction.

Rust `Prometheus` metrics with `prometheus` crate: `let counter = Counter::new("http_requests_total", "Total HTTP requests")?; let registry = Registry::new(); registry.register(Box::new(counter.clone()))?`. Increment: `counter.inc()`. Labels: `CounterVec::new(Opts::new("http_requests", "desc"), &["method", "status"])?; counter_vec.with_label_values(&["GET", "200"]).inc()`. Histogram: `Histogram::with_opts(HistogramOpts::new("latency_seconds", "desc").buckets(vec![0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0]))?`. Scrape endpoint: `let metric_families = registry.gather(); let mut buffer = Vec::new(); let encoder = TextEncoder::new(); encoder.encode(&metric_families, &mut buffer)?`. UNSAID: Use `prometheus` crate's `DEFAULT_REGISTRY` for simple cases; custom registry for library code (avoids polluting global namespace). Histogram bucket selection matters: too few buckets lose precision; too many increase cardinality. Standard Prometheus naming: `_total` suffix for counters, `_seconds` for time, `_bytes` for size, `_ratio` for ratios. Label values should be low-cardinality (status codes, methods) — never use user IDs or request IDs as label values.

Rust `map_err` and `?` operator type alignment: the `?` operator calls `From::from(err)` to convert error types. If no `From` impl exists between the error types, `?` fails to compile. Common failure: mixing `std::io::Error`, `serde_json::Error`, and custom errors in the same function body — need to convert all to a common type. Fix: either implement `From<IoError>` for `AppError` (via `#[from]` with `thiserror`), use `anyhow::Error` (accepts any `std::error::Error`), or use `.map_err(AppError::Io)?`. UNSAID: `Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync>` as the return type accepts any error — useful for scripts and `main()` functions: `fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync>>`. `?` in `main()` prints the error with Debug format and exits with code 1 — for user-facing error messages, print with Display before exiting. `eyre::Report` (from the `eyre` crate) provides better error display than `Box<dyn Error>` with cause chains and optional span trace.

Rust `integration testing` with `Testcontainers`: `testcontainers` crate manages Docker containers for tests — Postgres, Redis, Kafka spin up and tear down per test. `let docker = clients::Cli::default(); let postgres = docker.run(images::postgres::Postgres::default()); let db_url = format!("postgres://postgres@localhost:{}/postgres", postgres.get_host_port_ipv4(5432))`. `sqlx::migrate!().run(&pool).await?` applies migrations in the test. UNSAID: `testcontainers` requires Docker daemon running — CI must have Docker available (GitHub Actions has it by default). Container startup time (~2-5 seconds for Postgres) is amortized across all tests that share the container — use `once_cell::sync::OnceCell` for per-process container sharing. `testcontainers-modules` crate provides pre-built module configs for common services. For faster tests: use `sqlx`'s test isolation with transactions — wrap each test in a transaction and rollback on completion — avoids container restart between tests.

Rust `Iterator::scan` and stateful transformations: `iter.scan(initial_state, |state, item| { *state += item; Some(*state) })` — like `map` but with persistent state between iterations. Use for: running totals, sliding window calculations, stateful parsing. `fold` for reducing to a single value; `scan` for producing a new iterator with accumulated state. `Iterator::flat_map` vs `Iterator::flatten`: `flat_map(f)` is equivalent to `map(f).flatten()` — use `flat_map` when each element maps to an iterator. `Iterator::partition` splits into two vecs: `let (evens, odds): (Vec<_>, Vec<_>) = nums.iter().partition(|&&x| x % 2 == 0)`. `Iterator::unzip`: `let (keys, values): (Vec<_>, Vec<_>) = pairs.into_iter().unzip()`. UNSAID: `Iterator::zip` stops at the shorter iterator — use `itertools::zip_eq` to assert equal lengths or `itertools::zip_longest` for different-length iteration. `Iterator::step_by(n)` produces every nth element. `chain` concatenates two iterators of the same type without allocation.

Rust `UUID` generation and handling: `uuid` crate with features `["v4", "v7", "serde"]`. `Uuid::new_v4()` — random, no ordering. `Uuid::now_v7()` — time-ordered, sortable, better for database primary keys (reduces B-tree fragmentation). `uuid.to_string()` — hyphenated form; `uuid.simple().to_string()` — without hyphens. Parse: `Uuid::parse_str("550e8400-...")`. Database storage: `UUID` type in Postgres (16 bytes, indexed efficiently); `sqlx` has native `Uuid` support. UNSAID: UUIDv7 vs ULIDs: both are time-ordered — UUIDv7 is RFC standard (use for interoperability); ULID (`ulid` crate) is base32-encoded and URL-safe. UUIDv4 as primary key causes random B-tree inserts (page splits) — measured 20-30% slower inserts vs sequential IDs at scale. For Postgres: `gen_random_uuid()` (v4) is fine for low-volume tables; switch to UUIDv7 or sequences for high-volume insert tables.

Rust `std::process::Command` and output buffering: `Command::new("cmd").arg("arg").output()` collects all stdout/stderr into memory — problematic for commands producing large output (OOM). For large output: use `.stdout(Stdio::piped())` and `.spawn()`, then read the `ChildStdout` stream incrementally. `command.status()` only checks exit code (efficient for side-effect commands). Common failure: calling `command.output()` on a long-running process that produces continuous output — the process is blocked waiting for its stdout buffer to drain while Rust waits for it to exit. Fix: spawn + read concurrently. UNSAID: Deadlock risk: if a process writes to both stdout AND stderr, and both are piped, the buffers can fill. The process blocks waiting for the reader to consume output; the parent blocks waiting for the process to exit. Fix: spawn a thread to drain each pipe concurrently. `tokio::process::Command` provides async process execution — use `child.stdout.take()` and read via `AsyncReadExt` to avoid blocking the event loop.

Rust `tower-http` `TraceLayer` for structured HTTP logging: `Router::new().layer(TraceLayer::new_for_http().make_span_with(|request: &Request<_>| { info_span!("http_request", method = %request.method(), path = %request.uri().path(), version = ?request.version()) }).on_response(|response: &Response<_>, latency: Duration, _span: &Span| { info!(status = response.status().as_u16(), latency = ?latency, "response sent") }).on_failure(DefaultOnFailure::new().level(Level::ERROR)))`. Each request gets a unique span — nested database calls appear as child spans in traces. UNSAID: `TraceLayer` creates spans per-request. Add request ID to each span: `make_span_with` can read a `X-Request-ID` header and add it to the span. `on_request` fires before the handler; `on_response` fires after; `on_body_chunk` fires for streaming responses. For sensitive paths (health checks, metrics scrape): exclude from tracing to avoid log spam — use `tower_http::trace::TraceLayer::new_for_http().on_request(skip_health_checks_filter)`.

Rust `std::io` traits and buffering: `BufReader<R>` adds read buffering to any `Read` — reduces syscall count for many small reads. `BufWriter<W>` adds write buffering — essential for writing many small chunks (e.g., writing CSV line by line). `BufWriter::flush()` must be called explicitly or on drop — data in the buffer is NOT written until flushed. `io::Cursor<Vec<u8>>` implements both `Read` and `Write` on an in-memory buffer — use for testing code that accepts `Write` or for building bytes incrementally. `io::BufRead::lines()` iterator for line-by-line reading. UNSAID: `BufWriter` drop flushes the buffer but SILENTLY IGNORES flush errors — if the underlying write fails during drop, data is lost. Explicitly `flush()` before drop to catch errors. `io::sink()` discards all writes (like `/dev/null`); `io::empty()` reads zero bytes. `io::copy(reader, writer)` efficiently copies between any `Read` and `Write` using an internal buffer — prefer over manual read/write loops.

Rust `thread::spawn` and data sharing without Arc: `thread::spawn(move || { use(data) })` moves data into the thread — if you need the original, clone or use `Arc`. Common failure: calling `thread::spawn(|| { use(&local_var) })` without `move` — the closure borrows `local_var` but the thread may outlive the current scope, so the compiler rejects it. `thread::scope` (stable since Rust 1.63) allows scoped threads that can borrow from the enclosing scope: `thread::scope(|s| { s.spawn(|| { use(&local_var) }) })` — the scope blocks until all spawned threads complete, guaranteeing lifetime validity. UNSAID: `std::thread::Builder::new().name("worker").spawn(...)` names the thread — visible in debuggers, crash reports, and `top -H`. For CPU-intensive work: use `rayon` (work-stealing pool) rather than manually spawning threads. `thread::available_parallelism()` returns the number of logical CPUs — use for thread pool sizing. Thread panic is isolated — other threads continue running; use `thread::JoinHandle::join()` to propagate panics to the spawning thread.

Rust `minijinja` or `tera` for HTML/text template rendering: `tera`: `let mut tera = Tera::new("templates/**/*.html")?; let ctx = Context::from_serialize(&data)?; let html = tera.render("index.html", &ctx)?`. Auto-escaping: HTML entities escaped by default for `.html` templates. `minijinja`: `let env = Environment::new(); env.add_template("hello", "Hello {{ name }}!")?; let tmpl = env.get_template("hello")?; tmpl.render(context!(name => "World"))?`. Used in axum: return `Html(rendered_string)`. UNSAID: NEVER disable auto-escaping without explicit documentation — raw HTML injection via user-controlled template variables causes XSS. `{{ var | safe }}` in Tera/Jinja marks a variable as pre-escaped HTML — only use for HTML you generated yourself, never for user input. For email templates: `mrml` (MJML in Rust) for responsive email HTML. Template compilation at startup is preferred over runtime compilation — catches template syntax errors early and warms the regex/parser cache.

Rust `macro hygiene` and span information: proc macros produce `TokenStream` — spans carry source location info for error reporting. When generating code that references items from the calling crate: use `quote::quote!(crate::SomeType)` not `quote!(SomeType)` — the latter may not resolve in all contexts. For items from the proc macro's own crate: use `quote!(::my_proc_macro_helpers::Foo)` with the absolute path. UNSAID: Proc macros cannot see type information — they only see token streams, not resolved types. For type-aware code generation: use `build.rs` with type information from schema files, or use `inventory` / `linkme` crates for distributed registration patterns. `proc_macro2::Span::call_site()` for generated code spans — errors point to the macro invocation. `proc_macro2::Span::def_site()` for spans in the macro definition — used sparingly. Proc macro crates CANNOT be used as regular crates — they can only be used in `#[derive]`, `#[proc_macro]`, or `#[proc_macro_attribute]` positions.

Rust `s3` object storage with `aws-sdk-s3`: `let config = aws_config::load_defaults(BehaviorVersion::latest()).await; let client = aws_sdk_s3::Client::new(&config)`. Upload: `client.put_object().bucket(bucket).key(key).body(ByteStream::from(bytes)).content_type("application/json").send().await?`. Download: `let resp = client.get_object().bucket(bucket).key(key).send().await?; let bytes = resp.body.collect().await?.into_bytes()`. List: `client.list_objects_v2().bucket(bucket).prefix(prefix).send().await?`. Presigned URL: `client.presigned_url_config().put_object().bucket(b).key(k).presigned(PresigningConfig::expires_in(Duration::from_secs(300)))?`. UNSAID: `ByteStream::from_path` for streaming file uploads without loading into memory. `multipart_upload` for files > 5GB. AWS SDK uses connection pooling — reuse the `Client` across requests. Environment-based config: `AWS_REGION`, `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` OR instance profile/ECS task role. For local development: `localstack` + `endpoint_url("http://localhost:4566")` override.

Rust `async` and blocking I/O in tests: `#[tokio::test] async fn test_reads_file() { let content = std::fs::read_to_string("data.txt").unwrap() }` — `std::fs::read_to_string` blocks the thread, which is technically wrong in async context (blocks a tokio worker thread). For tests: this is usually acceptable as tests run one at a time. For production: use `tokio::fs::read_to_string`. More subtle: `std::net::TcpListener::bind().accept()` in tests blocks the tokio thread — use `tokio::net::TcpListener`. UNSAID: tokio's `current_thread` runtime (used by `#[tokio::test]` by default) has only one thread — blocking it completely prevents other tasks from progressing, unlike the multi-thread runtime. `#[tokio::test(flavor = "multi_thread")]` uses multiple threads for tests requiring concurrency. `tokio::task::block_in_place(|| std::fs::read(...))` is the correct way to block inside async tests.

Rust `clap` CLI argument parsing: `#[derive(Parser)] #[command(name = "myapp", about = "Description")] struct Cli { #[arg(short, long, default_value = "config.toml")] config: PathBuf, #[arg(short, long, action = ArgAction::Count)] verbose: u8, #[command(subcommand)] command: Commands }`. `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum Commands { Start { port: u16 }, Stop }`. Parse: `let cli = Cli::parse()`. Shell completions: `clap_complete::generate(Shell::Bash, &mut cmd, "myapp", &mut stdout())`. UNSAID: `clap`'s derive API is more ergonomic but slightly larger compile time than builder API. `value_parser` supports custom type parsing: `#[arg(value_parser = parse_duration)]`. `env` attribute reads from environment variable as fallback: `#[arg(long, env = "PORT", default_value = "3000")]`. For consistent CLI UX: `indicatif` for progress bars, `console` for terminal styling, `dialoguer` for interactive prompts.

Rust `bytes` crate for zero-copy network buffers: `Bytes` is a cheaply-cloneable reference-counted byte buffer. `BytesMut` is the mutable version. `Bytes::from_static(b"hello")` — no allocation. `buf.split_to(n)` splits off the first n bytes (zero-copy). `BytesMut::with_capacity(1024)` for building; `.freeze()` converts to immutable `Bytes`. Used by tokio, hyper, axum internally. `Buf` and `BufMut` traits from `bytes` enable reading/writing integers, strings from byte buffers without copying to intermediate vecs. UNSAID: `Bytes` uses `Arc<[u8]>` internally — cloning is O(1) (increments refcount). Multiple parts of the network stack can share the same underlying buffer without copying. `codec::Framed` in `tokio-util` wraps a `TcpStream` with a codec that uses `BytesMut` for framing. For high-performance message parsing: prefer `bytes::Buf::get_u32()` over manual index arithmetic — it handles endianness and advances the position.

Rust `trait` method collision and disambiguation: if a struct implements multiple traits with the same method name, calling the method requires disambiguation. `Trait1::method(&value)` or `<Type as Trait1>::method(&value)` for fully-qualified syntax. Common failure: implementing `Display` and a custom trait both defining `to_string()` — the ambiguity causes confusing compiler errors. Extension traits (adding methods to external types) can shadow existing methods if not careful: `MyExtension::new_method` should have unique names. UNSAID: `trait` method resolution order: inherent methods > trait methods. If you implement an extension trait on a type that gains the same method in a later version of the library, a conflict occurs. Use distinct method names for extension traits. `Deref` method resolution: methods of `T` shadow methods of `*T` — if `Box<T>` and `T` both have a `method()`, calling on `Box<T>` calls `T`'s method via Deref unless `Box<T>` defines it directly.

Rust `scheduled tasks` without tokio-cron: `tokio::time::interval` for fixed-rate tasks: `let mut interval = tokio::time::interval(Duration::from_secs(60)); loop { interval.tick().await; do_task().await }`. For cron-style scheduling: `tokio-cron-scheduler` or `croner` crates. `interval.set_missed_tick_behavior(MissedTickBehavior::Skip)` — if a tick is missed (task took longer than interval), skip the missed ticks and run at the next scheduled time. `MissedTickBehavior::Delay` — reschedule from when the task completed. `MissedTickBehavior::Burst` — catch up with all missed ticks immediately (risky: can overload downstream if many ticks missed). UNSAID: Periodic tasks should be idempotent — if two instances run simultaneously (e.g., during rolling deploy), the result should be correct. Use distributed locks (Redis `SET NX EX`) for tasks that must run exactly once. `tokio::time::interval_at(Instant, Duration)` starts the interval at a specific time — useful for aligning to clock boundaries (run at the top of each hour).

Rust `async fn` in traits and `dyn` compatibility: even with native async traits (Rust 1.75+), a trait with `async fn` methods is NOT automatically object-safe. `Box<dyn MyTrait>` with async methods requires boxing the returned futures. `trait_variant::make` macro (from `trait-variant` crate) generates a `Send`-bounded variant for use with `dyn`: `#[trait_variant::make(MyTrait: Send)] pub trait LocalMyTrait { async fn method(&self) -> Result<()> }`. The generated `MyTrait` has `fn method(...) -> impl Future<Output=Result<()>> + Send` — compatible with `Box<dyn MyTrait>`. UNSAID: This is the current state of async trait objects in Rust — there is no zero-cost solution for heterogeneous async trait dispatch. `async_trait` proc macro boxes every future (heap allocation per call) but works today. For performance-critical paths: use generics with `T: MyTrait` instead of `dyn MyTrait`. The Rust team is working on `dyn*` and async trait improvements — follow the blog for updates.

Rust `event sourcing` pattern: commands produce events; events are appended to an immutable log; state is derived by replaying events. `enum OrderEvent { Created { id: Uuid, items: Vec<Item> }, ItemAdded { item: Item }, Cancelled { reason: String } }`. State reconstruction: `fn apply(state: OrderState, event: &OrderEvent) -> OrderState { match event { OrderEvent::Created { id, items } => OrderState::Active { id: *id, items: items.clone() }, ... } }`. Persistence: store events as JSONB in Postgres with `event_type` and `aggregate_id` columns. Snapshots: periodically snapshot state to avoid replaying all events. UNSAID: `esrs` crate provides event sourcing building blocks for Rust. Event schema evolution is the hardest part — use `#[serde(rename_all)]` and `#[serde(default)]` for forward compatibility. `#[non_exhaustive]` on event enums prevents external crates from exhaustively matching — allows adding new variants without breaking downstream. Store events with a `version` (sequence number) per aggregate — enables optimistic concurrency control.

Rust `println!` and stdout buffering: `println!` writes to stdout with a lock, which is line-buffered by default. When stdout is redirected to a file or pipe, it may be fully buffered — `println!` doesn't flush between writes. For progress output (overwriting the same line): use `print!("{}", progress); io::stdout().flush()`. For high-throughput output: `let stdout = io::stdout(); let mut handle = stdout.lock(); writeln!(handle, "{}", line)?` — holds the lock across multiple writes, preventing interleaving in multi-threaded scenarios and reducing lock contention. UNSAID: `eprintln!` writes to stderr — unbuffered by default, always visible even when stdout is redirected. For structured output consumed by other programs: use JSON to stdout + human-readable log to stderr. `io::stdout().lock()` holds the mutex for the lock's lifetime — drop it promptly to avoid blocking other threads. `atty` crate (`is_terminal::IsTerminal`) detects if stdout is a TTY — disable color/progress output when not a terminal.

Rust `hyper` for low-level HTTP server/client: `hyper` is the foundation under `axum`, `reqwest`, and `warp`. Direct use for maximum control: `let listener = TcpListener::bind(addr).await?; loop { let (stream, _) = listener.accept().await?; let io = TokioIo::new(stream); tokio::spawn(async move { http1::Builder::new().serve_connection(io, service_fn(handle)).await }); }`. Service function: `async fn handle(req: Request<Incoming>) -> Result<Response<Full<Bytes>>, Infallible>`. Hyper 1.0 (2023): `hyper` is now split into hyper (core), `hyper-util` (utilities), and `http-body-util` (body helpers). UNSAID: Most applications should use `axum` instead of `hyper` directly — axum provides routing, extractors, and middleware. Use hyper directly for: building HTTP proxies, writing your own framework, maximum control over connection handling. `hyper` does NOT handle TLS — combine with `tokio-rustls` or `tokio-native-tls` for HTTPS. `hyper::client::conn` for connection-level HTTP client (for connection reuse control).

Rust `derive(Hash)` and mutability invariant: a type used as a `HashMap` key must not change its hash after insertion. If a struct's `hash()` depends on mutable fields and those fields are changed while the struct is a HashMap key, the entry becomes unreachable (it's in the wrong bucket). Rust does not prevent this — the HashMap stores keys by value (owned), so the key IS moved in and becomes immutable in the map. But if `K` contains `Cell<T>` or `RefCell<T>` (interior mutability), you could mutate the key after insertion by obtaining a reference to it. UNSAID: Never derive or implement `Hash` on types with interior-mutable fields that affect hash. `HashMap::keys()` returns references — if you somehow mutate through those references (unsafe code), you break the invariant. The safest approach: design hash keys as immutable data structs with no interior mutability. `indexmap::IndexMap::get_full_mut` returns a mutable reference to the VALUE but not the key — maintaining the invariant correctly.

Rust `kafka` integration with `rdkafka` (librdkafka bindings): `ClientConfig::new().set("bootstrap.servers", brokers).set("group.id", group).create::<StreamConsumer>()`. Subscribe: `consumer.subscribe(&["topic"])?`. Consume: `let message = consumer.recv().await?; let payload = message.payload_view::<str>().unwrap_or(Ok(""))?`. Commit: `consumer.commit_message(&message, CommitMode::Async)?`. Producer: `let producer: FutureProducer = ClientConfig::new().set("bootstrap.servers", brokers).create()?; producer.send(FutureRecord::to("topic").payload(msg).key(key), Duration::from_secs(5)).await?`. UNSAID: `rdkafka` wraps librdkafka (C library) — requires `cmake` and C compiler in build environment. `kafka-rust` is a pure-Rust alternative with less feature parity. Consumer group rebalancing: implement `CustomContext` with `pre_rebalance` to commit offsets before partition reassignment. Error handling: `KafkaError::MessageConsumption(RDKafkaErrorCode::UnknownTopicOrPartition)` — topic may not exist yet; retry with backoff.

Rust `std::cmp` utilities: `Ord::clamp(min, max)` constrains a value within bounds: `value.clamp(0, 100)`. `std::cmp::min(a, b)` and `std::cmp::max(a, b)` for two values. For custom ordering: implement `PartialOrd` and `Ord` via `#[derive]` (lexicographic on fields in declaration order) or manually. `Reverse<T>` wrapper reverses ordering: `BinaryHeap<Reverse<i32>>` becomes a min-heap. `std::cmp::Ordering`: `Less`, `Equal`, `Greater` — `cmp_fn.then_with(|_| other_cmp_fn())` for multi-key sorting. `Vec::sort_by_key(|x| x.field)` for simple key-based sorting; `sort_by(|a, b| a.partial_cmp(b).unwrap())` for floats (f64 is PartialOrd not Ord). UNSAID: `f64` does NOT implement `Ord` because `NaN != NaN` — you cannot directly sort `Vec<f64>`. Use `sort_by(|a, b| a.partial_cmp(b).unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal))` to treat NaN as equal, or use the `ordered-float` crate for `NotNan<f64>` which implements `Ord`. `sort_unstable` is faster than `sort` when equal elements can be in any order.

Rust `actix-web` patterns for high-performance HTTP: `#[actix_web::main] async fn main() -> io::Result<()> { HttpServer::new(|| App::new().service(index)).bind("127.0.0.1:8080")?.run().await }`. Route: `#[get("/users/{id}")] async fn get_user(path: web::Path<u64>, data: web::Data<AppState>) -> impl Responder { ... }`. Shared state via `web::Data<T>` (Arc-wrapped). Error handling: `impl ResponseError for AppError` to map errors to HTTP responses. JSON: `web::Json<T>` extractor deserializes body; `HttpResponse::Ok().json(&value)` serializes response. UNSAID: actix-web runs on tokio but has its own actor system (actix) for stateful actors — use `actix::Actor` for WebSocket handlers and stateful services. `actix-web` is benchmarked as one of the fastest Rust web frameworks due to actor-based architecture. `App::app_data(web::JsonConfig::default().error_handler(...))` customizes JSON parse error responses — default is a generic 400. For testing: `actix_web::test::TestRequest::get().uri("/").to_request()` with `actix_web::test::call_service`.

Rust `Box::new` allocation and stack overflow: placing large structs on the stack before boxing can cause a stack overflow on debug builds (stack frames are larger in debug mode). `Box::new(LargeStruct { ... })` — the struct is FIRST created on the stack, THEN moved to the heap. On debug builds, this intermediate stack allocation can overflow for very large structs (>1MB). Fix: `Box::try_new_zeroed()` (nightly) for zero-initialized large allocations directly on heap; or split large structs into smaller heap-allocated parts. UNSAID: In release mode, LLVM often optimizes away the stack intermediate — but this optimization is not guaranteed. `Vec::with_capacity(n)` + `extend` avoids stack intermediate for large collections. The issue appears in `main()` where stack size is the OS default (usually 8MB on Linux). Worker threads have configurable stack sizes: `thread::Builder::new().stack_size(32 * 1024 * 1024).spawn(...)` for threads requiring large stacks.

Rust `env_logger` structured logging setup: `env_logger::Builder::from_env(Env::default().default_filter_or("info")).format(|buf, record| { writeln!(buf, "{} [{}] {}", chrono::Local::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), record.level(), record.args()) }).init()`. For JSON output: use `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` with `JsonLayer` instead. `log::info!("User {} logged in", user_id)` — standard `log` crate macros work with `env_logger`, `tracing`, or any `log` implementation. `log::set_max_level(LevelFilter::Debug)` for dynamic adjustment. UNSAID: `env_logger` and `tracing-subscriber` both implement the `log::Log` trait — they're interchangeable backends. `tracing` is the modern choice for new code because it supports spans (structured context) not just messages. `log::log!(target: "db", Level::Debug, "query: {}", sql)` allows per-subsystem log targets for fine-grained filtering. For production: structured JSON logs are machine-parseable by log aggregators; include `timestamp`, `level`, `message`, `service`, `trace_id` fields.

Rust `nightly` features and stability guarantees: nightly-only features (behind `#![feature(...)]`) have no stability guarantees — they can change or be removed between nightly versions. Using nightly in production: acceptable if you pin to a specific nightly date (`rust-toolchain.toml: channel = "nightly-2024-01-15"`) and test before upgrading. Notable nightly features approaching stabilization: `let chains` (`let x = ...; let y = ...` in if conditions), `generic_const_exprs`, `type_alias_impl_trait`. UNSAID: `#![feature(something)]` requires nightly toolchain AND the feature gate — if you add a feature flag without nightly toolchain, you get a compile error explaining you need nightly. `rustup override set nightly` sets nightly for the current directory. `cargo +nightly build` uses nightly without permanently switching. Many proc macros that were nightly-only are now stable (derive traits, etc.). The `edition` and nightly features are orthogonal — you can use Rust 2021 edition on stable or nightly.

Rust `leptos` or `dioxus` for full-stack web: `leptos` uses reactive signals for UI: `let (count, set_count) = create_signal(cx, 0); view! { cx, <button on:click=move |_| set_count.update(|c| *c += 1)>{count}</button> }`. SSR: `leptos::ssr::render_to_string` for server-side rendering. `dioxus`: JSX-like macros, targets web/desktop/mobile. Full-stack: leptos server functions with `#[server]` — called from client, run on server, type-safe serialization via serde. Hydration: initial HTML from SSR, client JS hydrates. UNSAID: Both frameworks are less mature than React/Vue — production deployments exist but ecosystem is smaller. `trunk` is the Wasm bundler for Rust frontend: `trunk serve` for dev, `trunk build --release` for production. WASM bundle size: ~500KB gzipped for a basic leptos app (larger than equivalent React app but comparable to Next.js). For SEO-critical apps: SSR support in leptos is production-ready. Consider whether full-stack Rust (frontend + backend) is worth the investment vs TypeScript frontend + Rust backend.

Rust `lazy_static` and initialization order with global state: `lazy_static!` initializes on first access, in dependency order. If two `lazy_static` statics mutually initialize each other (rare but possible with closures), a deadlock occurs — the mutex protecting each is held while trying to initialize the other. `OnceLock` has the same deadlock risk for circular initialization. The issue is uncommon but insidious. Fix: avoid circular dependencies between global statics. Initialize complex globals explicitly in `main()` before spawning threads: `let db = init_db(); let cache = init_cache(&db); let app = build_app(db, cache)` then pass via `Arc<AppState>`. UNSAID: Global mutable state (`static mut`) requires `unsafe` and is inherently non-thread-safe — never use except in embedded contexts with single-threaded execution or interrupt handlers with proper critical sections. `thread_local!` provides per-thread storage — no synchronization needed: `thread_local! { static LOCAL: RefCell<Vec<u8>> = RefCell::new(Vec::new()) }; LOCAL.with(|v| v.borrow_mut().push(1))`.

Rust `axum` testing with `TestServer`: `axum_test::TestServer::new(app)?` starts the app in-process. `let resp = server.get("/users/1").await; resp.assert_status_ok(); let body: User = resp.json()`. No network overhead — tests run fast. `server.post("/users").json(&NewUser { name: "Alice" }).await`. With shared database: `let pool = PgPool::connect(&db_url).await?; let app = create_app(pool.clone()); let server = TestServer::new(app)?; sqlx::migrate!().run(&pool).await?`. Transaction isolation: wrap each test in a transaction that's rolled back on completion for full isolation. UNSAID: `axum_test` crate is the standard for axum integration testing. For testing auth-protected routes: `server.get("/protected").add_header("Authorization", format!("Bearer {token}"))`. `tower::ServiceExt::oneshot` (from axum's `TestRequest`) works without `axum_test` crate but is more verbose. Test databases: use separate database per test suite OR transaction rollback per test — never share mutable state between concurrent tests.

Rust `From/Into` trait conversions: `From<T>` for a type automatically provides `Into<T>` (blanket impl). `impl From<u32> for MyType { fn from(v: u32) -> Self { MyType(v) } }`. Infallible conversions use `From`; fallible use `TryFrom`/`TryInto`. `impl TryFrom<String> for Email { type Error = InvalidEmail; fn try_from(s: String) -> Result<Self, Self::Error> { ... } }`. The `?` operator uses `From` for error conversion. API design: accept `impl Into<T>` to allow callers to pass T or types that convert to T: `fn connect(url: impl Into<String>)` accepts `&str`, `String`, `Cow<str>`. UNSAID: `From<&str> for String` is in std — `String::from("hello")` works. `Into` is more ergonomic for callers: `fn f(x: impl Into<Url>)` vs `fn f(x: Url)`. Orphan rule applies: you can only implement `From<A> for B` if you own `A` or `B`. `#[from]` in thiserror auto-generates `From` implementations for error types — the main use case.

Rust `Arc::clone` vs clone: `Arc::clone(&arc)` and `arc.clone()` are equivalent — both increment the reference count. The idiomatic form `Arc::clone(&arc)` makes the intent explicit: 'this is a cheap reference count increment, not a deep clone'. Clippy enforces this with `clippy::clone_on_ref_ptr`. Common failure: `#[derive(Clone)] struct Service { db: Arc<Database> }` — calling `service.clone()` clones the struct, which means incrementing the Arc's refcount for `db`. This is fine and cheap. The confusion: some developers expect `service.clone()` to deep-clone the database connection. UNSAID: If a struct contains both `Arc<T>` (shared) and `Vec<U>` (owned) fields, `#[derive(Clone)]` clones the Arc (cheap) AND the Vec (copies all elements) — the overall clone cost is dominated by the owned data. Profile before assuming `clone()` is cheap. For structs that should share ALL their data: wrap the entire data in `Arc<InnerData>` and have the outer struct be just a newtype wrapper.

Rust `OpenAPI` generation with `utoipa`: `#[derive(ToSchema)] struct User { id: u64, name: String }`. Route documentation: `#[utoipa::path(get, path = "/users/{id}", responses((status = 200, body = User), (status = 404, description = "Not found")), params(("id" = u64, Path, description = "User ID")))]`. Aggregate: `#[derive(OpenApi)] #[openapi(paths(get_user, create_user), components(schemas(User, NewUser)))] struct ApiDoc`. Serve spec: `Router::new().route("/api-doc/openapi.json", get(|| async { Json(ApiDoc::openapi()) }))`. Swagger UI: `utoipa-swagger-ui` crate. UNSAID: `utoipa` generates OpenAPI 3.0 spec at compile time — no runtime overhead for generating the spec itself. Keep schema documentation up-to-date: add `#[schema(example = json!({"name": "Alice"}))]` for realistic examples in Swagger UI. `utoipa-axum` provides integration helpers that register both the route AND its documentation from a single `#[utoipa::path]` decorated handler.

Rust `std::sync::Condvar` for thread coordination: `let pair = Arc::new((Mutex::new(false), Condvar::new())); let (lock, cvar) = &*pair; let mut ready = lock.lock().unwrap(); while !*ready { ready = cvar.wait(ready).unwrap() } `. Producer: `let mut ready = lock.lock().unwrap(); *ready = true; cvar.notify_one()`. `wait()` atomically releases the lock and blocks — avoids the TOCTOU race of separate unlock/wait. `wait_timeout()` for bounded waits. `notify_all()` wakes all waiting threads. UNSAID: Condition variables require a paired `Mutex` — the wait ALWAYS re-acquires the mutex before returning. Spurious wakeups are possible (Condvar wakes up without notification) — always re-check the condition in a loop (the `while !condition { cvar.wait(guard) }` pattern). For async: prefer `tokio::sync::Notify` which is condvar-like but async-aware. `Condvar` is the correct primitive for producer-consumer with back-pressure in synchronous multi-threaded code.

Rust `serde_json::Value` and type coercion: `value["count"].as_i64()` returns `None` if the value is a JSON string `"42"` rather than number `42`. JSON numbers can be float or integer — `as_i64()` returns `None` for `3.14`. Parse JSON number to Rust integer: `value["count"].as_i64().ok_or("not an integer")?`. For JSON from APIs where types are inconsistent: use `#[serde(deserialize_with)] fn deserialize_flexible_int<'de, D>(d: D) -> Result<i64, D::Error>` with custom logic. UNSAID: `serde_json::Number` preserves whether a number was integer or float in the original JSON — `Number::is_i64()`, `is_f64()`. Very large integers in JSON may not fit in `i64` — use `u64` or `i128` for `Number::as_u64()`. For financial amounts: NEVER use JSON floats — use string representation of decimal or integer cents. `serde_with::DisplayFromStr` handles types that serialize as strings in JSON but are strongly typed in Rust (URLs, UUIDs, etc.).

Rust `migration` management with `sqlx-migrate`: `sqlx migrate add create_users` creates `migrations/TIMESTAMP_create_users.sql`. Up migration content: `CREATE TABLE users (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW())`. Down migrations are optional. Apply at startup: `sqlx::migrate!().run(&pool).await?` — idempotent, applies only pending migrations. `sqlx::migrate!()` macro embeds migrations at compile time (no runtime file access needed in containers). UNSAID: `_sqlx_migrations` table tracks applied migrations — never modify it manually. Migration files must be immutable after deployment: changing an applied migration causes `sqlx` to error on checksum mismatch. For rollbacks: write a new DOWN migration rather than editing the UP. Column renaming requires two migrations (add new, backfill, remove old) for zero-downtime deploys. `sqlx migrate info` shows migration status. Add an index in a separate migration from the table creation — large tables need `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` which cannot run in a transaction.

Rust `wasm32` target constraints: WASM has no threads by default (single-threaded execution model without SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics). `std::thread::spawn` is not available on `wasm32-unknown-unknown`. `Arc`/`Mutex` still compile but `Arc` is just `Rc` internally (no atomic ops needed). `tokio` doesn't run on `wasm32-unknown-unknown` — use `wasm-bindgen-futures` to bridge `js_sys::Promise` and Rust futures. `fetch` API via `web_sys::fetch_with_request` or `gloo_net::http::Request::get(url).send().await`. UNSAID: `wasm32-unknown-unknown` has no file system, no networking (must use browser APIs), no environment variables. WASM linear memory starts at 1MB and grows — `wasm-opt` (from binaryen) reduces WASM binary size by 20-30% after compilation. For WASM in Node.js: `wasm32-wasi` target has limited POSIX-like filesystem and networking. SharedArrayBuffer + `wasm-bindgen-rayon` enables threads in modern browsers — requires specific HTTP headers (`Cross-Origin-Isolation`).

Rust `tower::Service` composition for custom middleware: `struct LoggingMiddleware<S> { inner: S, logger: Logger }; impl<S, ReqBody> Service<Request<ReqBody>> for LoggingMiddleware<S> where S: Service<Request<ReqBody>, Response = Response<Body>> { type Response = S::Response; type Error = S::Error; type Future = S::Future; fn poll_ready(&mut self, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Result<(), Self::Error>> { self.inner.poll_ready(cx) } fn call(&mut self, req: Request<ReqBody>) -> Self::Future { self.logger.log(&req); self.inner.call(req) } }`. Simpler: `tower::service_fn(|req| async { ... })`. UNSAID: `poll_ready` MUST be called before `call` — this is the Tower contract. If `poll_ready` returns `Pending`, the service is not ready and `call` must not be called. Violating this causes UB in some service implementations. `tower::buffer::Buffer` wraps services that are not `Clone` — it buffers requests and dispatches them to the inner service via a channel. `tower::load_shed::LoadShed` returns errors instead of waiting when the service is not ready — useful for shedding load.

Rust `HashMap::entry` and borrow errors: the entry API holds a mutable borrow on the map — you cannot use the map while an entry reference is held. `let entry = map.entry(key); map.len()` — compile error: map is mutably borrowed. Common pattern: `map.entry(key).or_insert_with(|| compute_value())` — the closure runs only if the key is absent, avoiding a separate `contains_key` check. `entry.or_default()` inserts `Default::default()` if absent. `entry.and_modify(|v| *v += 1).or_insert(0)` — modify if present, insert 0 otherwise. UNSAID: `HashMap::get_or_insert` is NOT a thing — use the `entry()` API. The entry API is the ONLY way to do insert-or-update atomically (no TOCTOU race). For `BTreeMap`, the entry API works identically. `OccupiedEntry::get_mut()` returns a mutable reference to the existing value; `OccupiedEntry::insert(new_value)` replaces it and returns the old. Using `entry().or_insert_with` is more efficient than `if !map.contains_key(k) { map.insert(k, v) }` because it only hashes the key once.

Rust `minigrep`-style CLI tool structure: separate library crate from binary. `src/lib.rs` contains all logic; `src/main.rs` is thin: `fn main() { let config = Config::build(env::args()).unwrap_or_else(|e| { eprintln!("Error: {e}"); process::exit(1) }); if let Err(e) = run(config) { eprintln!("Error: {e}"); process::exit(1) } }`. Benefits: `lib.rs` is testable without spawning a process; `main.rs` handles OS integration. Integration tests: `tests/integration_test.rs` uses `use myapp::run`. UNSAID: `process::exit(1)` skips destructors — use only after logging errors. For complex CLIs: `clap` subcommands with `Router` pattern: each subcommand is a function returning `Result<(), Error>`. Error propagation in `main()`: `fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>` prints errors with `{:?}` debug format. For user-friendly errors: print with `{:#}` (pretty-printed Display with causes) from `anyhow::Error::chain()`.

Rust `std::net` socket programming: `TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080")?.incoming()` for synchronous TCP server. `TcpStream::connect("host:port")` for client. `UdpSocket::bind` + `.send_to` / `.recv_from` for UDP. `set_read_timeout(Some(Duration::from_secs(5)))` prevents blocking forever. `TcpStream::set_nodelay(true)` disables Nagle's algorithm for low-latency small messages. Socket options: `SO_REUSEADDR` via `socket2` crate for port reuse across restarts. UNSAID: For production servers: use `tokio::net::TcpListener` for async I/O. `std::net` is synchronous — each connection blocks a thread, limiting concurrent connections to thread pool size. `socket2` crate provides fine-grained control over socket options that `std::net` doesn't expose: `SO_KEEPALIVE` timing, `TCP_FASTOPEN`, `SO_LINGER`, receive/send buffer sizes. For high-performance UDP (game servers, video streaming): `tokio::net::UdpSocket` with async send/receive — avoids blocking the thread waiting for datagrams.

Rust `tokio::spawn` and large captured values: `tokio::spawn` moves the entire captured state into the task. For large data structures, this means copying `Vec<LargeStruct>` into the heap-allocated task. Common failure: capturing a large `Vec<_>` that's no longer needed after spawning — wastes memory until the task drops it. Fix: use `Arc<Vec<_>>` (shared reference) or clone only the needed subset. `tokio::spawn(async move { let _ = large_data; /* drop immediately */ rest_of_task.await })` — explicitly drop at the start of the task. UNSAID: Task size matters for performance — each spawned task allocates its future on the heap. A future containing a `[u8; 65536]` local array creates a 64KB heap allocation per task. Minimize local variables in async functions, especially large arrays — use `Box::new([0u8; 65536])` to heap-allocate explicitly. `tokio::task::spawn_local` for `!Send` types in a `LocalSet` — avoids the requirement for types to be `Send`.

Rust `password hashing` with `argon2` crate: `let salt = SaltString::generate(&mut OsRng); let argon2 = Argon2::default(); let hash = argon2.hash_password(password.as_bytes(), &salt)?.to_string()`. Verification: `let parsed_hash = PasswordHash::new(&hash_from_db)?; Argon2::default().verify_password(password.as_bytes(), &parsed_hash)?`. `Argon2::default()` uses `argon2id` variant with OWASP-recommended parameters. The PHC string format includes the algorithm, version, params, salt, and hash — self-describing and future-proof. UNSAID: argon2 is CPU and memory intensive by design — verification takes ~100ms with default params. This is intentional to slow brute force. For high-traffic login endpoints: use a separate goroutine/thread pool with bounded concurrency (max 10 concurrent bcrypt/argon2 verifications) to prevent DoS. Do NOT use `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` with an unbounded pool for password hashing — bounded concurrency is essential. Rate limiting login attempts at the API gateway level is the first line of defense.

Rust `event-driven` architecture with `tokio::broadcast` for pub-sub: `let (tx, _) = broadcast::channel(128)`. Subscriber: `let mut rx = tx.subscribe()`. Publish: `tx.send(event)?`. Multiple subscribers each receive a copy. Event types as enum: `#[derive(Clone, Debug)] enum AppEvent { UserCreated(User), OrderShipped(OrderId) }`. `rx.recv().await?` — returns `RecvError::Lagged(n)` if the subscriber fell behind and messages were dropped. UNSAID: `broadcast::channel` capacity is a ring buffer — when full, new sends cause old messages to be dropped, and slow subscribers get `RecvError::Lagged`.
