Set capacity based on expected message rate × maximum acceptable lag duration. For event sourcing (must not lose events): use `mpsc` with bounded channel and backpressure instead of broadcast. `select!` with multiple broadcast receivers: `tokio::select! { Ok(ev) = rx1.recv() => handle1(ev), Ok(ev) = rx2.recv() => handle2(ev) }`.

Rust `async recursion` stack overflow: a recursive `async fn` cannot be compiled directly — the future size is infinite (each call level nests the future). `fn fibonacci(n: u64) -> BoxFuture<'static, u64> { Box::pin(async move { if n <= 1 { n } else { fibonacci(n-1).await + fibonacci(n-2).await } }) }` — `BoxFuture` breaks the recursive size. Without boxing: the compiler gives 'recursive opaque type' error. `#[async_recursion]` proc macro (from `async-recursion` crate) automates the boxing: `#[async_recursion] async fn fibonacci(n: u64) -> u64 { ... }`. UNSAID: Async recursion is inherently heap-allocating — each recursive call allocates a new boxed future. For deep recursion: stacked futures grow the heap. Consider iterative approaches using explicit stacks (`Vec`) rather than recursive async functions for performance-critical paths. Tree traversal: convert recursive DFS to an iterative approach with a `Vec<Node>` stack — avoids both stack overflow and heap allocation per level.

Rust `lazy evaluation` with `Iterator::peekable` and `Iterator::take_while`: `let mut iter = data.iter().peekable(); while let Some(&&first) = iter.peek() { if first > threshold { break } iter.next() }`. For lazy stream processing: chain iterator adaptors — nothing is computed until consumed: `data.iter().filter(|x| expensive_predicate(x)).map(|x| expensive_transform(x)).take(10).collect::<Vec<_>>()` — processes at most 10 elements even if `data` is huge. `std::io::BufRead::lines()` is lazy — reads one line at a time from disk. UNSAID: `Iterator::by_ref()` allows partial consumption: `let first_three: Vec<_> = iter.by_ref().take(3).collect(); let rest: Vec<_> = iter.collect()` — the same iterator is reused for both. `Iterator::flatten` is lazy — it doesn't allocate intermediate collections. For infinite sequences: `(0..).filter(|n| n % 3 == 0).take(100)` — never materializes the infinite iterator. `std::iter::from_fn(|| Some(compute_next()))` creates a lazy iterator from a closure.

Rust `binary size` optimization for production deployments: default release builds include debug info. `Cargo.toml`: `[profile.release] strip = true  # strip symbols, lto = "fat"  # link-time optimization across crates, opt-level = "z"  # optimize for size (vs "3" for speed), codegen-units = 1  # single codegen unit for best LTO, panic = "abort"  # no unwinding, smaller binary`. Results: strip + LTO + abort can reduce binary size by 40-60%. UNSAID: `opt-level = "z"` vs `"s"` vs `"3"`: `z` optimizes for smallest size even if slower; `s` balances size/speed; `3` is maximum speed. For servers: use `opt-level = 3` (performance matters more than binary size). For CLI tools and embedded: `z` is often preferable. `upx --best` (runtime decompressor) can further compress binaries by 50-70% at the cost of startup time (~10-50ms decompression). `cargo-bloat` shows which functions contribute most to binary size — use to target optimization efforts.

Rust `WebSocket` server with `axum-websocket`: `async fn ws_handler(ws: WebSocketUpgrade, State(state): State<AppState>) -> impl IntoResponse { ws.on_upgrade(|socket| handle_socket(socket, state)) }`. `async fn handle_socket(mut socket: WebSocket, state: AppState) { while let Some(msg) = socket.recv().await { match msg { Ok(Message::Text(text)) => { socket.send(Message::Text(process(text))).await.ok() }, Ok(Message::Close(_)) => break, Err(_) => break, _ => {} } } }`. Ping/pong: axum handles ping/pong automatically — no manual handling needed. UNSAID: WebSocket connections are long-lived — each connection holds a socket and associated state. Memory usage scales linearly with concurrent connections. For 10K+ concurrent connections: ensure async I/O (not thread-per-connection), tune `ulimit -n`, and monitor heap usage. `socket.split()` separates sender and receiver halves — allows concurrent sending and receiving: `let (mut sender, mut receiver) = socket.split()`. Fan-out: store `Arc<Mutex<Vec<UnboundedSender<Message>>>>` in state to broadcast messages to all connected clients.

Rust `cargo test` parallelism and shared resources: tests within a single binary run in parallel by default. Common failures: (1) tests competing for the same port number — each test that binds to a port must use an ephemeral port or coordinate; (2) tests writing to the same file — use `tempfile::NamedTempFile` per test; (3) tests setting env vars — `std::env::set_var` is NOT thread-safe (UB in Rust's memory model when called concurrently); use `temp_env::with_vars` from `temp-env` crate which locks. Fix for ports: `TcpListener::bind("127.0.0.1:0")` binds to an OS-assigned ephemeral port; `listener.local_addr().port()` gets the assigned port. UNSAID: `cargo test -- --test-threads=1` serializes all tests — correct for globally stateful tests but slow. `#[serial_test::serial]` attribute (from `serial_test` crate) serializes only tests marked as serial while allowing others to run in parallel. For database tests: each test in its own transaction (rollback after test) is the gold standard for isolation + speed.

Rust `zero-downtime deployment` patterns: use `SO_REUSEPORT` + `SO_REUSEADDR` to allow multiple processes to bind to the same port. New process binds and starts accepting; old process drains in-flight requests then shuts down. With systemd: `Type=notify` + `WatchdogSec` for readiness notification and watchdog. With Kubernetes: `maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0` in rolling update ensures new pod is Ready before old is terminated. Axum graceful shutdown: `Server::with_graceful_shutdown(signal)`. `listenfd` crate accepts an already-bound socket from the supervisor (systemd socket activation or similar). UNSAID: `sd_notify(READY=1)` via `systemd::daemon::notify` signals readiness to systemd after all initialization (migrations, warm-up) completes. Without this signal, systemd may forward traffic before the app is ready. `WatchdogSec` requires periodic `sd_notify(WATCHDOG=1)` — implement as a background tokio task. Zero-downtime migration: add column as nullable, deploy app that handles both old/new, backfill, make column NOT NULL, deploy.

Rust `std::collections::BinaryHeap` for priority queues and scheduling: max-heap by default — largest element at top. `heap.push(item); heap.pop()` returns the maximum. For min-heap: `heap.push(Reverse(item)); heap.pop().map(|Reverse(v)| v)`. For complex priority: implement `Ord` on a wrapper struct. Dijkstra's algorithm: `BinaryHeap<(Reverse<Distance>, NodeId)>` — smallest distance first. `heap.peek()` inspects without removing. `heap.into_sorted_vec()` drains in descending order. `BinaryHeap::from(vec)` builds in O(n) (heapify) vs O(n log n) for repeated push. UNSAID: `BinaryHeap` does NOT support priority updates — to change an element's priority, you must remove and re-insert (requires tracking elements separately). Common pattern for graph algorithms: use 'lazy deletion' — push the updated entry, keep the old entry in the heap, ignore old entries when popped by checking if visited. `retain` is not available on `BinaryHeap` — drain and filter for conditional removal.

Rust `Arc<RwLock<T>>` write starvation: if read locks are held continuously by many readers, write lock requests may wait indefinitely (write starvation). `std::sync::RwLock` on Linux uses a read-preferring algorithm — writes can starve. `parking_lot::RwLock` uses a fair algorithm (FIFO acquisition order) — prevents write starvation. `tokio::sync::RwLock` is also reader-preferring by default; `tokio::sync::RwLock::write_owned()` for owned guard. For high read/write contention: consider `ArcSwap` for read-heavy workloads where writes are infrequent — reads are lock-free. UNSAID: `RwLock` poisoning: same as `Mutex` — a panicking writer poisons the lock. `parking_lot::RwLock` doesn't poison. Upgrading from read to write lock (`try_upgrade`) is supported in `parking_lot` but not `std` — attempting upgrade in std requires releasing the read lock first (TOCTOU window). `tokio::sync::RwLock::read()` must be dropped before acquiring write — holding both causes deadlock.

Rust `pagination` API pattern with cursor-based pagination: prefer cursor over offset for large datasets (offset pagination degrades as offset grows — O(offset) query cost). Cursor: last seen ID or encoded sort key + ID. `SELECT * FROM items WHERE id > $1 ORDER BY id LIMIT $2` — next cursor is the last returned ID. Encode cursor as base64 of JSON: `base64::encode(serde_json::to_string(&cursor)?)`. Return: `{ items: [...], next_cursor: Option<String> }` — `null` when no more pages. With `sqlx`: `query_as::<_, Item>("SELECT * FROM items WHERE id > $1 ORDER BY id LIMIT $2").bind(cursor_id).bind(page_size + 1)` — fetch N+1, if N+1 returned, there's a next page; cursor is item N's ID. UNSAID: Cursor pagination is not sortable by arbitrary fields without compound cursors. For arbitrary sort: encode `(sort_field_value, id)` as cursor to handle ties. Rate limit page size: cap at 100 items maximum. Use `i64::MAX` as initial cursor for 'start from beginning'. Total count with cursor pagination requires a separate `COUNT(*)` query — often omitted for performance.

Rust `std::mem` utilities: `mem::size_of::<T>()` compile-time type size; `mem::size_of_val(&x)` runtime size of a value (important for slices/trait objects). `mem::swap(&mut a, &mut b)` swaps two values without cloning. `mem::replace(&mut val, new_val)` sets a new value and returns the old one — useful in `Option` patterns: `mem::replace(&mut opt, None)` = `opt.take()`. `mem::forget(val)` leaks memory without running `Drop`. `mem::transmute::<T, U>(val)` reinterprets bits as different type — requires same size, entirely unsafe. UNSAID: `mem::transmute` is one of the most dangerous operations in Rust — use only when you have absolute proof of type compatibility. Safer alternatives: `bytemuck::cast` for plain-data types with `Pod` (Plain Old Data) derive. `mem::zeroed::<T>()` creates a zero-initialized T — safe for types where all zeros is a valid state, undefined behavior for types with non-zero validity invariants (like `bool` which must be 0 or 1, not 2). Use `Default::default()` instead.

Rust `cargo publish` and feature flag compatibility: `cargo publish` checks that all feature combinations compile — if `--features a` and `--features b` work individually but `--features a,b` fails, cargo publish detects this with `--all-features`. Common failure: features that assume exclusivity — `#[cfg(feature = "backend-a")] fn connect() {}` and `#[cfg(feature = "backend-b")] fn connect() {}` — enabling both causes duplicate function definition. Fix: use `#[cfg(all(feature = "backend-a", not(feature = "backend-b")))]` or restructure to avoid conflicts. `cargo check --all-features` in CI catches this before publish. UNSAID: `cargo publish --dry-run` shows what files would be included — verify `.gitignore` doesn't accidentally exclude needed files. `include` key in `Cargo.toml` explicitly lists files to include in the crate package — more reliable than relying on `.gitignore`. Max crate size on crates.io is 10MB compressed — large asset files (test fixtures, data files) should be excluded from the package.

Rust `once_cell::sync::Lazy` for global HTTP clients: `static HTTP_CLIENT: Lazy<reqwest::Client> = Lazy::new(|| reqwest::Client::builder().timeout(Duration::from_secs(30)).user_agent("service/1.0").build().expect("failed to build HTTP client"))`. Access: `HTTP_CLIENT.post(url).json(&body).send().await?`. The client is initialized once on first use, thread-safely. Similarly for database pools: `static DB_POOL: Lazy<PgPool> = Lazy::new(|| { tokio::runtime::Handle::current().block_on(async { PgPool::connect(&env::var("DATABASE_URL").unwrap()).await.unwrap() }) })`. UNSAID: `block_on` inside `Lazy::new` is an antipattern in async code — it creates a nested runtime issue if called from within an async context. Prefer passing the pool/client via application state (`Arc<AppState>`). Static globals are fine for configuration-only data and truly global clients (no request-scoped state). For tests: static globals make test isolation harder — tests share the global state. Use per-test setup where possible.

Rust `Display` trait implementation for user-facing output: `impl fmt::Display for Temperature { fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result { write!(f, "{:.1}°C", self.celsius) } }`. For enums: match on variants. `fmt::Display` enables `format!("{temp}")`, `println!("{temp}")`, `.to_string()`. `impl fmt::Debug for T` for developer output — `#[derive(Debug)]` is usually sufficient. Custom width/fill: `f.width()` and `f.fill()` for alignment: `write!(f, "{:>10}", self.value)`. `fmt::LowerHex`, `fmt::UpperHex`, `fmt::Binary`, `fmt::Octal` for numeric display formats. UNSAID: Implementing `fmt::Display` automatically implements `ToString` via a blanket impl — `my_val.to_string()` works without separate impl. Never implement `Display` to output machine-readable data — use `Serialize` (serde) instead. `write!` returns `fmt::Result` — `?` propagation works inside `fmt()` implementations. For conditional debug output: `if cfg!(debug_assertions) { eprintln!("{:?}", expensive_debug_info) }` — excluded in release builds.

Rust `tokio::fs` and directory traversal: `tokio::fs::read_dir(path).await?` returns an async `ReadDir`. `while let Some(entry) = dir.next_entry().await? { let path = entry.path(); let metadata = entry.metadata().await? }`. Common failure: using `std::fs::read_dir` in async code — it's synchronous, blocks the event loop. `tokio::fs` wraps all `std::fs` operations asynchronously. For recursive directory traversal: `walkdir` crate (synchronous) or `async-walkdir` (async). UNSAID: `tokio::fs` operations call `spawn_blocking` internally — they use the blocking thread pool, not async I/O. True async file I/O requires `io_uring` on Linux (via `tokio-uring` crate) — `tokio::fs` is not zero-cost async. For most applications this distinction doesn't matter. `tokio::fs::File` open options: `OpenOptions::new().read(true).write(true).create(true).open(path)`. Atomic file writes: write to temp file, then `fs::rename(temp, target)` — rename is atomic on POSIX systems.

Rust `circuit breaker` pattern with `failsafe-rs` or manual implementation: `enum State { Closed, Open { until: Instant }, HalfOpen }`. Transitions: Closed → Open after N failures in window; Open → HalfOpen after timeout; HalfOpen → Closed on success, Open on failure. `struct CircuitBreaker { state: Mutex<State>, failure_count: AtomicU32, threshold: u32, timeout: Duration }`. `async fn call<F, Fut, T>(cb: &CircuitBreaker, f: F) -> Result<T, CbError> where F: FnOnce() -> Fut, Fut: Future<Output=Result<T, Error>> { match cb.state() { State::Open { until } if Instant::now() < until => Err(CbError::Open), _ => { match f().await { Ok(v) => { cb.on_success(); Ok(v) }, Err(e) => { cb.on_failure(); Err(CbError::Inner(e)) } } } } }`. UNSAID: Implement per-downstream service, not globally — one slow service shouldn't trip the circuit for unrelated services. `half-open` state allows exactly ONE request through to probe recovery — use `AtomicBool::compare_exchange` for single-request enforcement. Circuit breaker metrics: emit `circuit.state` (closed/open/half-open) as a gauge to detect and alert on open circuits.

Rust `cargo` build cache and CI optimization: `sccache` (shared compilation cache) dramatically speeds up CI by caching compiled artifacts across builds. Configure: `RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache cargo build`. Cache backends: S3 for shared CI cache, local disk for developer machines. `cargo build --timings` shows per-crate compile times — identifies bottlenecks. `Dockerfile` caching: use `cargo-chef` to cache dependency compilation separately from application code — only application code is recompiled on source changes. UNSAID: Rust CI compilation time is dominated by dependencies on first run — subsequent runs with sccache can be 5-10x faster. `CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0` in CI disables incremental compilation — incremental adds per-artifact overhead that's counterproductive in full clean builds. `RUSTFLAGS='-C link-arg=-fuse-ld=lld'` (Linux) or `mold` linker reduces link time significantly (~50-70% faster linking than GNU ld). `cargo nextest` parallelizes test execution better than `cargo test` — typically 20-40% faster test runs.

Rust `openssl` vs `rustls` TLS choice: `rustls` is a pure-Rust TLS implementation — no OpenSSL dependency, easier cross-compilation, no C library. `reqwest` with `default-features = false, features = ["rustls-tls"]` uses rustls. `tokio-rustls` for low-level TLS in tokio servers. `openssl` (via `openssl` crate) is necessary for: FIPS compliance, client certificates with HSMs, or environments mandating OpenSSL. `native-tls` uses the platform's TLS library (SChannel on Windows, SecureTransport on macOS, OpenSSL on Linux) — best for interoperability with platform certificate stores. UNSAID: `rustls` does NOT support TLS 1.0/1.1 (intentionally — they're insecure). If you must support legacy clients: use `openssl`. Rustls uses the `webpki` crate for certificate validation — the CA bundle is compiled in (not system CAs by default). Add `webpki-roots` feature or load system CAs explicitly for production. Alpine Linux doesn't have OpenSSL by default — `apk add openssl-dev` or switch to `rustls`.

Rust `#[non_exhaustive]` and matching: `#[non_exhaustive] pub enum Status { Active, Inactive }` — external crates CANNOT exhaustively match this enum without a wildcard arm `_ => {}`. This prevents the 'semver hazard' of adding a new variant being a breaking change. Common failure: library authors not using `#[non_exhaustive]` on public enums — adding a new variant is a breaking change requiring a major version bump. For structs: `#[non_exhaustive]` prevents external crates from constructing the struct with `{ field1: val, field2: val }` syntax — must use a constructor function. UNSAID: `#[non_exhaustive]` only affects external crates — within the defining crate, exhaustive matching still works. For API versioning: `#[non_exhaustive]` is the correct tool for forward-compatible enums. The downside: external code cannot optimize with exhaustive matching (no compiler guarantee that all cases are handled). Libraries should document which variants are stable and which may be added.

Rust `diesel` ORM patterns: `#[derive(Queryable, Selectable)] #[diesel(table_name = users)] struct User { id: i32, name: String, email: String }`. Query: `users::table.filter(users::email.eq(email)).select(User::as_select()).get_result(&mut conn)?`. Insert: `#[derive(Insertable)] #[diesel(table_name = users)] struct NewUser { name: String, email: String }; diesel::insert_into(users::table).values(&new_user).execute(&mut conn)?`. `diesel_migrations::embed_migrations!()` embeds SQL migration files. Connection pool: `diesel::r2d2::Pool` (sync) or `deadpool-diesel` (async). UNSAID: Diesel works with synchronous connections — for async use `diesel-async` crate with `AsyncConnection`. Diesel's DSL is type-safe at compile time — invalid queries are compile errors. The schema file (`schema.rs`) is auto-generated from the database by `diesel setup` + `diesel migration run`; commit it to version control. Diesel supports Postgres, MySQL, SQLite — choose `features = ["postgres"]` appropriately.

Rust `flume` channel for MPMC: `flume` provides multi-producer multi-consumer channels, unlike `std::sync::mpsc` (single consumer) or `tokio::sync::mpsc` (single consumer). `let (tx, rx) = flume::bounded(100); tx.send(msg)?; rx.recv()?`. Both `tx` and `rx` are `Clone` — multiple producers AND multiple consumers. Async: `tx.send_async(msg).await?; rx.recv_async().await?`. `flume` is a zero-dependency, thread-safe, high-performance channel. UNSAID: `crossbeam-channel` is another MPMC option — slightly faster than `flume` in benchmarks but heavier dependency. `flume` works in both sync and async contexts seamlessly. For work distribution across multiple workers: `let (tx, rx) = flume::bounded(WORKERS * 2); for _ in 0..WORKERS { let rx = rx.clone(); tokio::spawn(async move { while let Ok(job) = rx.recv_async().await { process(job).await } }) }`. `rx.drain()` consumes all available messages without blocking — useful for batch processing.

Rust `trait impl` coherence (orphan rules): you can only implement a trait for a type if you own either the trait OR the type (or both). `impl Display for Vec<String>` — error: both `Display` and `Vec` are external. Common workaround: newtype wrapper: `struct MyVec(Vec<String>); impl Display for MyVec { ... }`. This applies to `From`, `Into`, `Serialize`, `Debug` — any external trait on external types. UNSAID: The newtype pattern has ergonomic cost — you must wrap and unwrap constantly. `Deref<Target=Vec<String>>` on the newtype gives transparent access to `Vec` methods. For `serde`: `#[serde(with = "module")] field: ExternalType` allows custom ser/deser without a newtype wrapper — define `serialize` and `deserialize` functions in `module`. `serde_with::serde_as` macro is more ergonomic: `#[serde_as] struct Foo { #[serde_as(as = "Vec<DisplayFromStr>")] items: Vec<IpAddr> }`.

Rust `Notify` and `Barrier` for task coordination: `tokio::sync::Notify` for one-shot signaling: `let notify = Arc::new(Notify::new()); notify.notify_one()` wakes one waiting task; `notify.notify_waiters()` wakes all. `let notify2 = notify.clone(); tokio::spawn(async move { notify2.notified().await; proceed() })`. `tokio::sync::Barrier::new(n)` for N-way synchronization: all tasks call `barrier.wait().await` and all are released together when N tasks have reached the barrier. UNSAID: `Notify` has no backpressure — if `notify_one()` is called when no tasks are waiting, the notification is stored and will be consumed by the NEXT `notified().await`. This is a 'permit' semantics: one outstanding notification allows one future waiter to proceed immediately. `Barrier` is useful for phased parallel computation: all workers complete phase 1 before any starts phase 2. Unlike `std::sync::Barrier` (panics on thread joining), `tokio::sync::Barrier` cleanly handles tasks that are cancelled.

Rust `associated types` vs generics tradeoff: associated types (`type Output` in a trait) enforce one implementation per type; generics allow multiple implementations. `Add<Rhs=T>` has a generic `Rhs` allowing `impl Add<f32> for Vector` and `impl Add<Vector> for Vector`. `Iterator` has associated `type Item` — a type can only be an iterator over one item type (not both `Item=u32` and `Item=String`). Use associated types when there's a naturally unique relationship; use generics when multiple relationships make sense. UNSAID: `where T: Iterator<Item = String>` in trait bounds is more ergonomic than `where T: Iterator, T::Item = String`. Associated type bounds in trait definitions: `trait Processor { type Output: Debug + Send }`. The `async_fn_in_trait` feature (nightly) uses associated types internally for async methods. `type Assoc<T>: Debug` (generic associated types / GATs, stable in Rust 1.65) allows lifetime-parameterized associated types — essential for streaming iterators.

Rust `distributed tracing` context propagation: W3C TraceContext standard (`traceparent`, `tracestate` headers) for HTTP. `tracing-opentelemetry` + `opentelemetry-http` handles injection/extraction: `let cx = global::get_text_map_propagator(|p| p.extract(&HeaderExtractor(req.headers())))`. In outbound requests: `global::get_text_map_propagator(|p| p.inject_context(&cx, &mut HeaderInjector(headers)))`. `reqwest-tracing` crate auto-injects trace context into `reqwest` HTTP calls. For async context propagation within a service: `tracing::Span::current()` + `span.in_scope(|| ...)` or `#[tracing::instrument]`. UNSAID: Without context propagation, traces appear as disconnected spans — each service shows isolated spans but they're not connected in the waterfall view. The `traceparent` header format: `00-<trace_id>-<span_id>-<flags>`. `Baggage` (part of W3C) carries custom key-value pairs through the trace — use for `user_id`, `tenant_id` that should appear in all spans. `opentelemetry::Context::current_with_span()` for programmatic context management.

Rust `macro_rules` and repetition pitfalls: `$(expr),+` matches one-or-more comma-separated items; `$(expr),*` matches zero-or-more. Trailing commas: `vec![1, 2, 3,]` works because vec! uses `$(expr),*`. Common failure: macro that doesn't handle trailing comma: `my_macro!(a, b, c,)` — the trailing comma causes a parse error. Fix: use `$(expr),* $(,)?` pattern to optionally accept a trailing comma. Mixing `tt` and specific matchers: `($a:expr, $b:expr)` requires EXACTLY two expressions with a comma; `($($x:expr),+)` accepts one or more. UNSAID: `macro_rules!` rules match in order — put more specific patterns first. `$x:expr` greedily matches — it will consume `a + b` as a single expression. Use `$x:tt` for token-by-token matching when you need to inspect the tokens. Recursive macros: must have a termination base case: `macro_rules! my_macro { () => {} ($x:expr $(,$rest:expr)*) => { process($x); my_macro!($($rest),*) } }`. Metavariable kinds: `expr`, `stmt`, `ty`, `ident`, `path`, `pat`, `block`, `meta`, `tt`, `lifetime`.

Rust `multi-tenant` application patterns: isolate per-tenant data via Row Level Security in Postgres or separate schemas. Rust side: `struct TenantContext { tenant_id: Uuid }` in `AsyncLocalStorage`-equivalent via `tokio::task_local!`. `task_local! { static TENANT_CTX: TenantContext }`. Set in middleware: `TENANT_CTX.scope(ctx, handler(req, state)).await`. Access in any async fn: `TENANT_CTX.with(|ctx| ctx.tenant_id)`. Postgres RLS: `SET app.tenant_id = 'uuid'` per connection, with RLS policy `USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::uuid)`. UNSAID: `task_local!` in tokio is scoped to the current task — spawned tasks do NOT inherit the context. Must be re-set in `tokio::spawn` blocks if needed. Connection pool with tenant context: use transaction-scoped `SET LOCAL app.tenant_id` (reverts on transaction end) rather than session-scoped `SET` to avoid context leaking between requests sharing a pooled connection. Test multi-tenancy isolation: write tests that explicitly verify data is NOT visible across tenant boundaries.

Rust `std::io::Error` and OS error code loss: wrapping errors with `io::Error::new(kind, msg)` creates a new error with a string message but LOSES the original OS error code. `io::Error::last_os_error()` captures the current OS errno. Common failure: `fs::open(path).map_err(|e| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::NotFound, "file missing"))` — the original OS error (including `errno`, path info) is lost, making debugging harder. Fix: use `.map_err(|e| io::Error::new(kind, e))` to chain the original error as the source, or just use `?` which preserves the original error. `io::Error::other(e)` (Rust 1.74+) for wrapping non-io errors. UNSAID: `io::Error.os_error()` returns `Option<i32>` — the raw OS errno. For cross-platform error handling: match on `io::ErrorKind` (not os error codes). `io::ErrorKind::WouldBlock` for non-blocking I/O; `io::ErrorKind::Interrupted` for signal interruption (should retry). `std::io::Result<T>` is `Result<T, io::Error>` — the standard return type for I/O operations.

Rust `async trait` mocking with `mockall`: `#[cfg_attr(test, automock)] #[async_trait] pub trait UserRepository { async fn find_by_id(&self, id: u64) -> Result<Option<User>, DbError> }`. Test: `let mut mock = MockUserRepository::new(); mock.expect_find_by_id().with(eq(1)).returning(|_| Ok(Some(user_fixture()))). times(1); let service = UserService::new(Box::new(mock)); service.process_user(1).await?`. UNSAID: `mockall::automock` with `async_trait` requires both attributes. `mockall` generates a `MockTraitName` struct. `times(1)` asserts the method is called exactly once — if not called or called multiple times, the test panics on drop. `returning(|args| ...)` must return the type including `Ok`/`Err` wrapping. For static methods: `MockMyTrait::static_method_context()` with `mockall::Sequence` for ordering. Avoid mocking concrete types — only mock through trait interfaces (enables dependency inversion principle).

Rust `production readiness checklist` for axum/tokio services: (1) graceful shutdown with SIGTERM handler; (2) health endpoints `/health/live` and `/health/ready`; (3) structured JSON logging with `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber`; (4) Prometheus metrics endpoint `/metrics`; (5) OpenTelemetry tracing with trace context propagation; (6) database connection pool with health checks; (7) request timeout middleware; (8) CORS configuration; (9) rate limiting; (10) panic handler that returns 500 instead of crashing. `tower_http::catch_panic::CatchPanicLayer::new()` converts panics to 500 responses. UNSAID: 'It compiles and the tests pass' is not production readiness. Operational concerns: How does it fail? How do you know it's failing? How do you recover? The `tracing` + OpenTelemetry + Prometheus triad covers observability. Graceful shutdown + health probes cover Kubernetes lifecycle. Rate limiting + timeouts cover resilience against slow or malicious clients. Panic handler prevents a single bad request from crashing the process.
